issue_title: Leaving and re-entering a room can cause incorrect room state
issue_body:
It seems there is an issue with `BaseManager.pending_removals` getting stale and causing the removal of a user from a room they should be in.  

Example (Imagine the various leave/enter/emit calls being spread out over time and  multiple requests):

```python
server = ... # Socket.IO Server
room = 'my room'
session_id = ... # Current session id for one user

# Enter room normally
server.enter_room(session_id, room)  

 # Leave room normally, Manager.pending_removals populated
server.leave_room(session_id, room) 

# Re-enter room, Manage.pending_removals still populated
server.enter_room(session_id, room)     

# Message emitted, followed by pending_removals being processed internally
server.emit('something', 'data', room=room)     

# Message *NOT* emitted, because the room was removed after the previous emit
server.emit('something else', 'data', room=room)
```

The workaround was to do:

```python
server = ... # Socket.IO Server
room = 'my room'
session_id = ... # Current session id for one user

# Enter room normally
server.enter_room(session_id, room)  

 # Leave room normally, Manager.pending_removals populated
server.leave_room(session_id, room) 

# WORKAROUND: Force clean-up of old removals before entering the new room
server.manager._clean_rooms()

# Re-enter room, Manage.pending_removals is now clear
server.enter_room(session_id, room)     

# Message emitted normally
server.emit('something', 'data', room=room)     

# Message emitted normally
server.emit('something else', 'data', room=room)
```

This leads me to believe that the fix is likely something that needs to be added to `enter_room( ... )` that reconciles the new subscriptions to a given room with the previous, perhaps unreconciled, room evictions in `BaseManager.pending_removals`.

issue_title: Unit tests should not execute Python subprocess
issue_body:
The unit test suite currently executes a Python interpreter in a sub-process. This should not be necessary, as the functionality to be tested is available by just calling a function — and this makes for a more tightly-defined test case.

Coupling the narrowly-defined test cases to a whole subprocess is fragile, and results in issues like issue #98.

Instead, the test cases should each invoke just a function in the ‘xkcd_password’ library, and test the result of calling that function.

issue_title: TypeError: build_attrs() takes from 1 to 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
issue_body:
Hi, I'm experiencing the below error using django v1.10.2:

```
Django Version: 	1.10.2
Exception Type: TypeError
Exception Value: build_attrs() takes from 1 to 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
Exception Location: .../bootstrap_datepicker/widgets.py in render, line 115
```

The culprit seems to be the below line as `build_attrs` doesn't expect `attrs`:
```
input_attrs = self.build_attrs(attrs, extra_attrs)
```

Replacing the above with:
```
if attrs:
    self.attrs.update(attrs)
input_attrs = self.build_attrs(extra_attrs)
```
seems to work.

issue_title: Wrong bootstrap column calculations
issue_body:
As per object, on Bootstrap's *col-xs* devices (expecially when running Chrome) there's a small overlap between these two big main divs, *#content* and *#sidebar-menu*. This is preventing both correct website usage and sidebar menu hiding (the show-hide-sidebar-menu button falls behind the sidebar-menu div and hence it's not visible).

I've noticed this is due to a wrong match between the number of columns assigned to sidebar div and the number of columns assigned to the content div when dealing with *col-xs*-sized devices. Here's the code:

**bootstrap_admin/templates/admin/base.html:68**
`<div id="sidebar-menu" class="col-xs-6 col-sm-3 col-md-2 sidebar-menu">`

**bootstrap_admin/templates/admin/base.html:83**
`<div id="content" class="{% if is_displaying_menu %}col-xs-9` [...]

As you can see, with col-xs-6 and col-xs-9 we're having a total of 16 columns here... A bit too many :)

I've fixed that by replacing the "9" with another "6", which as far as I have seen leads to the best result. Any combination with a total of 12 would of course work as well. In any case, we have to accordingly replace as well the value here:

**bootstrap_admin/static/bootstrap_admin/js/base.js:31**
`main_classes = 'col-xs-9 ` [...]

In my case, I've put a "6" there as well, as it has to match the one in *base.html:83*.

Hope this helps.
Cheers.

issue_title: MNIST example hangs on GPU, CHD 5.11, Spark 2.1, Yarn
issue_body:
Hello,

I have observed python processes allocated GPU (nVidia M60), however GPU utilization is 0% and processes are frozen. I was running MNIST example (feed_dict).  The TensorFlowOnSpark from September 25.  tensorflow-1.4 (compiled) or tensorflow-gpu-1.3 (from pip) both wirth Cuda support have same behavior. 

MNIST example is running fine on GPU directly from tensorflow.

Thanks for any suggestion.

Nvidia driver: 384.73
Cuda: 8.0.61

issue_title: How to handle exceptions when using SPARK mode which run tensorflow in backgroud process
issue_body: some exceptions in tensorflow function will make  backgroud process exit, but the spark thread does not know the situation and will be blocked in the queue operations.

issue_title: django_heroku overwrites my STATIC_URL setting to my S3 bucket.
issue_body:
Not sure if I'm missing anything but when I set my STATIC_URL to my s3 bucket, STATIC_URL changes back to '/static/'. 

Line 90 of core.py in django_heroku is where the STATIC_URL is reset. 

Is this a bug or am I just missing something?

issue_title: no:flaky instructions in README don't work
issue_body:
Docs say:

> With py.test, flaky will automatically run. It can, however be disabled via the command line:
        py.test no:flaky


but when I run it pytest shows an error:

``` ERROR: file not found: no:flaky ```

issue_title: WebSocket server shouldn't postpone ping for server side send
issue_body:
The last_data is updated when server sends any data to client. And WebSocket server uses this timestamp to decide if it should send a ping.

This actually defeats the purpose of the ping.

The ping is used to check if the client is still responsive. Server sending data doesn't mean client is good

issue_title: Improve test suite to be less flaky using port 0
issue_body:
Right now the test suite picks a port for Daphne to listen on for each test and runs it, and sometimes the ports clash. Rather than doing this, we should do what Django's `LiveServerTestCase` does and instead listen to port 0 and let the OS pick us a free port.

The one complication is we'll need a way for Daphne to communicate back with the port it's listening on. I'd suggest parsing its console output for now, it'll still be better than what we have.

issue_title: Direct SSL is not sent to Django
issue_body:
When you start up Daphne with SSL enabled and generate absolute URLs in your Django application, the resulting URLs does not use HTTPS, but HTTP.

This results in unusable URLs.

issue_title: Very High Load
issue_body:
I'm trying to develop a new websocket server with channels.
I need to have a websocket connection with each of my clients (IOT cards) 
I find  very high cpu rates with daphne.
**Configuration debian 9.4:**
asgiref (2.3.0)
channels (2.1.0)
channels-redis (2.1.1)
defusedxml (0.5.0)
Django (1.11.12)
In channels we use WebsocketConsumer or AsyncWebsocketConsumer

In Channels
```
class OcppJA(AsyncWebsocketConsumer):
    groups = []

    async def connect(self):
        await self.accept()

    async def websocket_receive(self, text_data=None, bytes_data=None):
        text = json.loads(text_data['text'])
        borne = self.scope['url_route']['kwargs']['borne']

        status = "enabled"

        payload = {"status": status, "test1": 3600, "test2": 3600}
        msg = json.dumps([3, text[1], payload])
        await self.send(text_data=msg)
```

With 500 clients whe have 70% CPU load (1core)
With 750 client swhe have 100% CPU load (1core)
Customers just connect and wait messages
In my old weksocket server (python 3 + Autobahn + Twisted) we have very low cpu rates .
For example 3% with 1000 clients

Do you have an idea or maybe this product is not suitable for my use

issue_title: User-Agent not being set
issue_body:
The User-Agent header is not being set as expected. Using the example below I see the User-Agent:
`python-requests/2.10.0` not the expected `python-cloudant/2.0.0/Python/2.7.11/Darwin/x86_64`

- Cloudant (python-cloudant) version(s) that are affected by this issue.
2.0.0

- Python version
2.7.11

- A small code sample that demonstrates the issue.
```python

issue_title: NDArray invocations with scalar arguments.
issue_body:
There are many complaints about passing scalar arguments to MXNet's ndarray calls (https://github.com/dmlc/minpy/issues/139, https://github.com/dmlc/minpy/issues/134). What I am thinking about is to have a wrapper for every registered NDArray function in MXNet such that if the given argument is scalar but supposed to be array, do an automatic conversion. My question is should we do it on python side? or on C++ side?
- If done on python side, each ndarray call will have an extra wrapper (another function redirect), which may introduce overhead.
- I don't know whether this is supported on C++ side, since I guess C++ cannot have arguments of either NDArray or scalar type?

@piiswrong @hotpxl @lryta

issue_title: Different prediction when manually creating batch
issue_body:
Hey everyone,

I'm using mxnet-cu80 0.11.0 under Debian 8.
I was playing around with adversarial perturbations using mxnet and I noticed some strange behavior.
I have a batch which comes from an ImageIter which reads a .rec file and for this batch, the classifier seems to do the right prediction, namely for the first example in the batch I get label 956. However, when I create my own batch in the following way, the classifier predicts a different label for the first data point:

   ```
    #this batch comes from the ImageIter 
    batch_data = batch.data[0]
    batch_label = batch.label[0].asnumpy()
    batch_size = batch_label.size
    _, _, img_size, _ = batch_data.shape

    #this is the new batch
    perturbed_data = mx.nd.zeros(batch_data.shape)
    #it should be all zeros, except for the first image in the batch 
    perturbed_data[0] = batch_data[0]
    dataDesc = [mx.io.DataDesc('data', (batch_size, 3, img_size, img_size), layout='NCHW')]
    new_batch = mx.io.DataBatch([perturbed_data], provide_data=dataDesc)

    #forward pass on original batch
    mod.forward(batch, is_train=True)
    old_probs = mod.get_outputs()[0].asnumpy()
    old_preds = np.argmax(old_probs,1)

    #forward pass on new batch
    mod.forward(new_batch, is_train=True)
    new_probs = mod.get_outputs()[0].asnumpy()
    new_preds = np.argmax(new_probs,1)

```
For the original batch, I get the output [956 577 201  64 412 307 314 141  54 641], whereas I get
[479 845 845 845 845 845 845 845 845 845] for the new batch, so I was wondering why the classifier predicts a different class in this case and how I could fix this behavior? I also tried removing the dataDesc, however this does not influence the classification. 

Thanks,
Max

issue_title: Badly formatted string inputs cause ValueError instead of SwaggerMappingError
issue_body:
Given a `string` value that has a `date-time` format, an incorrectly formatted input (say, "string") will cause `dateutil.parser.parse` to raise `ValueError: Unknown string format` when unmarshalling the request.  This isn't checked anywhere, so it bubbles up as an unexpected error, rather than being reported as invalid input.

It seems like the place to check this is in `unmarshal_primitive`: the call to [formatter.to_python](/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/unmarshal.py#L72) could be wrapped in a try block, with `ValueError` trapped and re-raised as a `SwaggerMappingError` (allowing callers to recognize this as a malformed request)—is that the right fix?

issue_title: unintuitive behavior when a param does not have a value
issue_body:
A request such as `example.com/path_to_resource?bool_flag=` where the type of `bool_flag` is `boolean`, the value of `bool_flag` would be set to `False` and if the type was `integer` or `number` the value would be set to `''` (empty string). This behavior seems arbitrary and unintuitive.

I propose their values be set to `None`, as no value was provided. A caveat however, string types would remain `''` (empty string) because there is no other way to specify an empty string as a param's value.

issue_title: Set application_name databse options by default in shell_plus
issue_body:
On production (and other) system it may be important to identify SQL queries origin, there is a mecanism in PostgreSQL called **application_name** I suggests to automatically set this in *shell_plus*, it may be set two in other Database System. It's easy to set this by adding an **OPTIONS** in `settings.DATABASES`, I'll propose a PR to do this

With right log configuration you'll see lines like theses in your logs :

```
[4-1] user=rodo,db=foo,host=10.0.10.1,app=shell_plus LOG:  duration: 50.004 ms  statement: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM bar
[5-1] user=rodo,db=foo,host=10.0.10.1,app=worker1 LOG:  duration: 45.434 ms  statement: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM bar WHERE id = 42
[6-1] user=rodo,db=foo,host=10.0.10.2,app=worker2 LOG:  duration: 42.998 ms  statement: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM bar WHERE id = 37
```

The first two lines just defers by the **application_name** (parameter %a in `log_line_prefix` postgres config entry), as is you can  immediatly know that bad query without a where clause was done by a human using the *shell_plus* and it's not in your code.
 
Ref :  http://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/static/libpq-connect.html#LIBPQ-CONNECT-APPLICATION-NAME

issue_title: S101 flags trailing comma after kwargs in function call
issue_body:
Given the below example

```python
def foo(*args, **kwargs):
    print(args, kwargs)


foo(
    1,
    2,
    **{
        'a': 3,
    }
)
```

`flake8-strict` will ask for a trailing comma

```
$ flake8 example.py
example.py:8:5: S101 Multi-line construct missing trailing comma
```

If I add one

```diff
--- example.py	2017-05-05 13:33:11.000000000 -0700
+++ example-update.py	2017-05-05 13:33:44.000000000 -0700
@@ -7,5 +7,5 @@
     2,
     **{
         'a': 3,
-    }
+    },
 )
```

I get a syntax error

```
$ flake8 example-update.py
example-update.py:10:6: E999 SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

I see the trailing comma is ignored for `**kwargs` in a function declaration [here](https://github.com/smarkets/flake8-strict/blob/c20b69fbe7e7370fc25ed39f288c5d375ec11db6/test_data.py#L20). It would make sense to not ask for a trailing comma in this scenario, too

issue_title: sops 3.0.0 updates all encrypted values even if only one line is modified
issue_body:
sops 1.16 written in Python has the feature that you can modify 1 key in a json/yaml file and only that value would be encrypted again, leaving a clean git history showing which values were modified in each revision.   sops 3.0.0 does not do this, if run I `sops filepath` and modify only one key's value and save sops re-encrypts every value in the tree making it difficult to see what changed at each revision.  Same happens when using the `--set` flag.

In doing this blanket decrypt/re-encrypt of the entire file, yaml indentation and in file comments are also lost in the process, which does not happen in sops 1.16.

See `git diff`s demonstrating this:

issue_title: Use a shared HTTP/HTTPS session() for retrieving data
issue_body: As of now we seem to make individual connections to the remote host for querying every tiny information and finally downloading the build. It would be better to share those connections via a session (http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/advanced/#session-objects). It can result in a more or less good speed improvement.

issue_title: Handle next URL parameter to redirect after login
issue_body:
It's common when handling logins to django to pass an extra ``next`` URL param to define where user should be redirected after successful login. In our case

* On authentication initialization we should optionally get ``request.GET["next"]`` and store it in user session (if it exists)
* On callback we should pop this from the session and redirect user after successful login.

```python
class Tree(collections.defaultdict):

issue_title: Async Trait Observers
issue_body:
I'm really just throwing this idea out there to see if there's any demand for it. Perhaps this would be useful for changes that result in a network request? I haven't really thought about it too deeply though.

cc: @rgbkrk @SylvainCorlay @ellisonbg @minrk @tacaswell 

```python
import asyncio
import random


class AsyncTrait(object):
    
    def __set__(self, obj, value):
        task = asyncio.ensure_future(obj.handle(value))
        obj._tasks.append(task)
    
    def __get__(self, obj, cls):
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*obj._tasks))
        return obj._x


class HasAsyncTraits(object):
    
    a = AsyncTrait()
    
    def __init__(self):
        self._tasks = []
        self._x = 0
    
    async def handle(self, value):
        await asyncio.sleep(random.random())
        self._x = value

        
obj = HasAsyncTraits()

for i in range(10):
    obj.a = i

# computation occurs now
print(obj.a)
```

issue_title: Luigi keep implicitly retry failure tasks  - after task is disabled
issue_body:
Hi everyone, 

We're using luigi 2.7.1.
We configured a retry policy which define three retries in one hour before  disabling the task for 1200 seconds.
After the suspension (disabled) time of 1200 seconds the task was expected to ran again only if it was explicitly called and not being implicitly  retry by the worker-scheduler retry policy.

We noticed that after the task is marked from the diable status (after 1200 sec) to the pending status -  the Luigi retry the task again.
This scenario of marking the failure task as disabled and after the 1200 second suspension, keep retry for the pending task continue without ever stopping.

Is there a way to mark a disabled task that finish the disable suspension to run only if it was explictly called and not retry the task implicitly ?? 

Following is our configuration:
[scheduler]
retry_delay=60
retry_count=3
disable-hard-timeout=21600
disable-persist-seconds=1200

[worker]
timeout=3600
keep_alive=True
count_uniques=True

issue_title: Order of evaluation bug in ObjFileParser.cpp
issue_body:
Hello, just want to say thank you this awesome tool, currently using it for my game engine =)

I encountered an error though, when starting my engine with the latest Assimp (clone), and after searching for a while I pin-pointed the error to *ObjFilerParser.cpp:182*.
This bug was introduced in a recent commit: https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blame/9969167c2a81a3460362a8319809f8c094c0d725/code/ObjFileParser.cpp#L182

The problem lies within this function call:
```c
getFace(*helperIt == 'f' ? aiPrimitiveType_POLYGON : (*helperIt == 'l'
                    ? aiPrimitiveType_LINE : aiPrimitiveType_POINT), ++helperIt, helperItEnd);
```
the statement
```c
getFace(..., ++helperIt, ...);
```
will be evaluated before
```c
getFace(*helperIt == 'f' ? aiPrimitiveType_POLYGON : (*helperIt == 'l'
                    ? aiPrimitiveType_LINE : aiPrimitiveType_POINT), ..., ...);
```
thus causing the type to be aiPrimitiveType_POINT even if the current character is 'f' (aiPrimitiveType_POLYGON).

To verify this one can move the ternary statement and store the result in a temporary just before the function call, everything should work as expected then.

I'm using GCC 5.2.1 on Ubuntu 15.10 x86_x64

I apologize if I sound too harsh/direct, this is my first issue report and english is not my native tounge.

issue_title: Worker processes can't use multiprocessing (indirectly prevents usage of scikit-learn and nltk)
issue_body:
This is a spin off issue from the Celery issue [#1709 Tasks are not allowed to start subprocesses](https://github.com/celery/celery/issues/1709). The Problem was [first mentioned by ostrokach ](https://github.com/celery/celery/issues/1709#issuecomment-122467424) who provided a stack trace that occures when he uses a scikit-learn classifier inside a worker. Since the problem seems to be cause by billiard and not by Celery itself I'm creating a new issue here.

### Stacktrace
```
[2015-07-17 21:23:51,299: ERROR/MainProcess] Task app.f[329d0da4-2e0e-4e1f-8148-d64f47750b1f] raised unexpected: AttributeError("'Worker' object has no attribute '_config'",)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 240, in trace_task
    R = retval = fun(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 438, in __protected_call__
    return self.run(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/username/working/playground/celery/app.py", line 11, in f
    clf = pickle.load(open('clf.pickle', 'rb'))
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/sklearn/ensemble/__init__.py", line 6, in <module>
    from .base import BaseEnsemble
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/sklearn/ensemble/base.py", line 13, in <module>
    from ..externals.joblib import cpu_count
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/sklearn/externals/joblib/__init__.py", line 112, in <module>
    from .parallel import Parallel
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/sklearn/externals/joblib/parallel.py", line 23, in <module>
    from ._multiprocessing_helpers import mp
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/sklearn/externals/joblib/_multiprocessing_helpers.py", line 25, in <module>
    _sem = mp.Semaphore()
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/multiprocessing/context.py", line 81, in Semaphore
    return Semaphore(value, ctx=self.get_context())
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/multiprocessing/synchronize.py", line 127, in __init__
    SemLock.__init__(self, SEMAPHORE, value, SEM_VALUE_MAX, ctx=ctx)
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/multiprocessing/synchronize.py", line 59, in __init__
    kind, value, maxvalue, self._make_name(),
  File "/home/username/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/multiprocessing/synchronize.py", line 117, in _make_name
    return '%s-%s' % (process.current_process()._config['semprefix'],
AttributeError: 'Worker' object has no attribute '_config'
```

### To reproduce
Create an empty classifier and save it as a pickle:

```
import pickle
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
clf = GradientBoostingClassifier()
pickle.dump(clf, open('clf.pickle', 'wb'))
```
Create a simple app (`app.py`):
```
import pickle
import sklearn
from celery import Celery

app = Celery(__name__, broker='amqp://localhost//')

@app.task
def f():
    print('hello')
    clf = pickle.load(open('clf.pickle', 'rb'))
    print(clf)
```
Start the celery worker:
```
celery -A app worker --loglevel=debug
```
Run the app:
```
python -c "from app import f; f.delay()"
```
---
I myself also ran into the same error while importing the functions from NLTK (a popular Natural Language Processing library). The problem **only** exists when the code in question is actually run as a worker. It always the problem that the value that is returned by `multiprocessing.current_process()` has no `_config` attribute.

The current workaround for this specific error is:
```
from celery.signals import worker_process_init

@worker_process_init.connect
def fix_multiprocessing(**kwargs):
    from multiprocessing import current_process
    try:
        current_process()._config
    except AttributeError:
        current_process()._config = {'semprefix': '/mp'}
```
However, I'm totally not sure what this might break elsewhere.

---

To me, it looks like the problem was introduced by c7eedbd0ee1498e76d4fa1affac5b1a275660ee7. Prior to that the [`Worker` class](https://github.com/celery/billiard/commit/c7eedbd0ee1498e76d4fa1affac5b1a275660ee7#diff-42cbdfeee4eeeb2688454e975c2d18ffL238) was derived from [`Process`](https://github.com/celery/billiard/blob/master/billiard/process.py#L77) which indeed hat an appropriate `_config` attribute.

issue_title: Celery incompatible with Ansible
issue_body: When using both Billard (by Celery) and the standard process.py module (by Ansible), there are some compatibility issues because they both define _current_process, but they define it differently. In particular, the standard process.py tries to get _current_process._authkey, but this is not an attribute of the Billard version of the _current_process which stores the authkey in  _current_process._config['authkey']

issue_title: boto3 unable to read in resources when run from an egg
issue_body:
We are running boto3 by appending all required eggs to `PYTHONPATH`. However, when we call `session.get_available_resources()` it returns `[]`. Through a bit of debug, we realized that the JSON service files were not being read in from the `<BOTOCORE_ROOT>/data` directory. We actually were getting a **NotADirectoryError** when directly calling `os.listdir(<BOTOCORE_ROOT>/data)`.

This is because `BOTOCORE_ROOT` is initialized by reading in `__file__` and according to [this site](http://peak.telecommunity.com/DevCenter/PythonEggs#accessing-package-resources), 

> [W]hen code like this is packed inside a zipfile, it can no longer assume that \_\_file__ or \_\_path__ contain filenames or directory names, and so it will fail.

In order to support compressed installation, it is suggested to use `pkg_resources.resource_filename()` instead of `__file__`.

This seems to be an issue that other people have also run into ( #332 #308) and was hoping it could be path in a future version. Or, I would love to know some alternative solution to getting an s3 resource from a boto3 session.

issue_title: Misleading boto3 datetime response syntax and structure description.
issue_body:
Currently all the boto3 documentation show date element as datetime() object.e.g.  
```
{
    'Contents': [
        {
            'Key': 'string',
            'LastModified': datetime(2015, 1, 1),
            'ETag': 'string',
  .....
}
```
As show in above example, `LastModified` actually contains a STRING value, which generate from python datetime() function.  This misleading information continue under response structure description. e.g. 
**LastModified (datetime) --**

This will mislead use who use JMES filter to pass datetime object value, then the formatted date time string. 

Suggested fix : 

1. Add quote for all datetime related element, e.g.
 ```
{
    'Contents': [
        {
            'Key': 'string',
            'LastModified': 'datetime(2015, 1, 1)',
            'ETag': 'string',
  .....
}
```
2. Elaborate how the datetime string format
**LastModified (string) -- ** Formatted date and time string of which data is uploaded, date format  'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'

issue_title: `ec2.images.filter()` doesn't raise exception when trying to access AMI that user can't access
issue_body:
Take this example:

```python
region = 'us-west-1'
ami = 'ami-fba6bd9a'

ec2 = boto3.resource(service_name='ec2', region_name=region)
image = list(ec2.images.filter(ImageIds=[ami]))[0]
```

If you run this, the call to `filter()` will raise `botocore.exceptions.ClientError`, where the exception error code is `InvalidAMIID.NotFound`.

However, if you change `region` to `us-west-2`, the same call to `filter()` just returns an empty result set. This is because the AMI exists but you don't have permission to access it.

Is that expected? It seems wrong since it leaks information about the existence of the AMI. What I would expect instead is to get the same exception as before.

issue_title: SQS message batch send IDs really required?
issue_body:
According to
http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/APIReference/API_SendMessage.html
MessageId is a response from SQS. However when I want to batch send messages, according to the docs here:
http://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/guide/sqs.html
I have to specify an Id. Why? If multiple programs are writing into the queue the Id may not be unique anymore. If boto3 needs it why can't it generate an Id by itself like when I send single messages? In any case if boto3 uses it internally and it has nothing to do with me or SQS's MessageId, why should I have to make sure that my data structures contain this field?

issue_title: Intermittent WSAECONNABORTED when uploading file to s3
issue_body:
Hi,

When uploading large files (using **multipart** upload) to s3 under **windows 7** using boto3, I am intermittently getting **WSAECONNABORTED** errors. 

The exception that is raised is **OpenSSL.SSL.SysCallError** (with the message: 10053, 'WSAECONNABORTED).

My understanding is that botocore does not retry uploading the failing part when this exception is raised because this exception is not part of the _retryable_exceptions collection, member of MaxAttemptsDecorator, filed up from the EXCEPTION_MAP collection (**retryhandler.py**) and is thus only caught by boto3 in transfer.py when it is already too late. At that time, **S3UploadFailedError** is raised. At this point, the only options are to catch the exception and start the upload over or not use the high level API offered by boto3.

I am posting here because it appears that handling this in the boto3 layer would be too late.

Please find below a snippet of code to reproduce the issue that allows me to consistently reproduce the issue (the file I upload is around 150MB):

```
import boto3

# Please initialize the following variables with the proper values.
region_name = ''
aws_access_key_id = ''
aws_secret_access_key = ''
local_file_pathname = ''
bucket_name = ''
s3_key = ''

client = boto3.client(
    service_name='s3',
    region_name=region_name,
    aws_access_key_id=aws_access_key_id,
    aws_secret_access_key=aws_secret_access_key
)

config = boto3.s3.transfer.TransferConfig(
    multipart_threshold=8 * 1024 * 1024,
    max_concurrency=20
)

transfer = boto3.s3.transfer.S3Transfer(client, config)
for i in range(1, 25):
    transfer.upload_file(
        local_file_pathname,
        bucket_name,
        s3_key)

```

Note: Centos7 boxes on the same network do not exhibit the problem.

Thank you for your time,

Best regards,
Alexis

issue_title: Why is there a StringProcessor class?
issue_body:
Reading the source code, I was confused as to why there is a `StringProcessor` class defined in `string_processing.py`?  You never initialize it, and it contains only static methods; in fact, even the `replace_non_letters_non_numbers_with_whitespace()` can be made static by using the `@staticmethod` decorator. It appears like it is used entirely as a namespace.

Is there some extra reasoning behind this design that I missed? Or could the same thing be achieved  by just making all static methods free functions in a module?

EDIT: There is an [answer on Stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/a/18262662/1889530) discussing functions which do not have a shared state.

issue_title: Templates not found in sub-directories (add_jinja2_search_path )
issue_body:
When you add a directory to the jinja-search path normally, like this:

``` python
config.add_jinja2_search_path('pyramid_jinja_issue:templates', name='.html')
```

then Pyramid/Jinja won't find the template when it's located in a sub-directory. This one works:

``` python
@view_config(route_name='works', renderer='some_template.html')
def my_view(request):
    ...
```

but this one doesn't work:

``` python
@view_config(route_name='broken', renderer='email/other_template.html')
def my_view(request):
    ...
```

Providing the full asset-specification works again, but the other way based on the search-path should work, too.

Example to reproduce here (simply try the 3 different routes `/works`, `/works2`, `/broken`:
https://github.com/martinstein/pyramid_jinja_issue

issue_title: Pre-generate thumbnails to improve performance
issue_body:
Currently, thumbnails are generated at run-time as needed using the `thumbnail` tag. Each time this tag is used, first the app must check if the thumbnail already exists on the media storage, and if not, generate it. When the media storage is remote such as on S3, this can take a lot of time.

It would be much more efficient to instead use the `thumbnail_url` tag which assumes the thumbnail already exists and just writes the appropriate url in the HTML. This just means the thumbnail should be pre-generated whenever the `thumbnail_options` for an image are modified.

There could be a switch to enable both the current dynamic and the pre-generating behavior, but since I don't see any advantage for the dynamic approach in the context of this package, I suggest just changing the behavior to always pre-genrate the thumbnails.

issue_title: Multiple shell commands in pipenv script
issue_body:
Scripts should allow multiple shell commands to be run as one "script". Both Yarn and npm allow this. Currently, if you try to do this:

```
[scripts]
foo = "echo 'bar' && echo 'baz'"
```

it will result in `bar && echo baz`. You can sidestep this issue by doing something like

```
[scripts]
foo = "bash -c \"echo 'bar' && echo 'baz'\""
```

but
1. that's a bit inconvenient
2. it isn't a cross-platform solution (it won't work on Windows)

The use case that I have is running `coverage report` after running coverage, but it's definitely something that would have many general uses.

------------

##### Expected result

Running `pipenv run foo` should result in:

```
bar
baz
```

##### Actual result

Running `pipenv run foo` results in:

```
bar && echo baz
```

##### Steps to replicate

Create a Pipfile with the following:

```
[scripts]
foo = "bash -c \"echo 'bar' && echo 'baz'\""
```

issue_title: "development philosophy" link in CONTRIBUTING.md is broken?

issue_title: --deploy only checks for Pipfile/Pipfile.lock consistency (ignoring setup.py dependencies)
issue_body:
Having a Pipfile as:

```
[[source]]

url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]

"e1839a8" = {path = ".", editable = true}

[dev-packages]

"e1839a8" = {path = ".", editable = true, extras=["dev"]}
```

After the Pipfile.lock file is generated, if we modify dependencies in `setup.py` and do a `pipenv install --deploy` it happily installs the dependencies without noticing the dependencies in the source have changed.

The reason is because the `pipfile.hash` [property](https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/blob/master/pipfile/api.py#L96,L99
) checks only for Pipfile contents but, in our case our dependencies are defined in `setup.py`.

For us, expected result would be the command failing because dependencies in `setup.py` and `Pipfile.lock` don't match.

For reference, part of the code where `--deploy` option is implemented: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/f5b482453f545fee58e3a4322bd9e7759db85f9f/pipenv/cli.py#L1270

Haven't found any issue related so sorry in advance if this is already documented/discussed somewhere

issue_title: Pipenv install doesn't copy data defined in `package_data` (setup.py)
issue_body:
##### Issue description

Hello folks, I created a simple package for demonstration purposes and included some data with the `package_data` directive (in my setup.py). The package was working fin both on linux and Mac, but wasn't copying over the data files on Windows. I fixed it by including a MAINFEST.in file and an `__init__.py` file in my directory. But the standard solution wasn't working fine.

Here's the code: https://github.com/santiagobasulto/party-parrot/tree/0.0.2

I've fixed my command in 0.0.3 with the above mentioned hack, so use 0.0.2 for testing.

##### Expected result

To copy the data and work well like in Linux and Mac.

##### Actual result

Data files were not copied.

##### Steps to replicate

Using windows:

```bash
$ pipenv install party-parrot==0.0.2  # this version is the one with issues
$ pipenv shell
$ party
```

(this works with `party-parrot@0.0.3` but not with `party-parrot@0.0.2`.

issue_title: Pip version in the Pipfile.lock
issue_body:
Bundler is an amazing tool in ruby-land.
I hope you guys study some of it, as it works really really well.
Specifically, A major pain point there was an addition of the specific "Bundler" version to the Gemfile.lock .

This was a painful migration, but later on allowed much more power to the Bundler authors, if it's in upgrades or in specific lock file changes.
I think if you add this and the specific bundler logic (only update when newer version exists) it will save much pain later on - 
https://github.com/bundler/bundler/issues/3697

issue_title: sys.path ordering differences between 18.7.1 to 36.0.1
issue_body:
I'm a Buildroot user and after bumping setuptools to 36.0.1 we now experience issues with Python 2.7.x and packages that need a CFFI backend. You can find more details on the [CFFI mailing list](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/python-cffi/ttSTbCpnDEo/7dkPw8fOAQAJ).

Buildroot (BR) has so called host-python, that is compiled for the host BR is running on and target-python, that is part of the target root file system. During target package setup we augment PYTHONPATH with target/usr/lib/python2.7/sysconfigdata and target/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages, so that setup.py could have access to other target python packages.

In the case of CFFI it is important, that _cffi_backend will be imported in the host-variant otherwise we get:

ImportError: /home/peko/autobuild/instance-2/output/target/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_cffi_backend.so: wrong ELF class: ELFCLASS32

In setuptools version 18.7.1 put cffi-1.10.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg before our target/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages, so that host-variant could be imported, but setuptools 36.0.1 makes sys.path ordering differently and cffi-1.10.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg lands at the end of the list.  The ordering can be taken from the above mentioned discussion on the CFFI mailing list.

How can one enforce another sys.path order?

issue_title: egg_info command must write setup.cfg stanza idempotentally
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **warsaw (Bitbucket: [warsaw](http://bitbucket.org/warsaw), GitHub: [warsaw](http://github.com/warsaw))**

----------------------------------------

The setup.py sdist command runs the egg_info command which will write an [egg_info] section to your existing setup.cfg file.  However, the keys in this file are not written in a predictable order, and are in fact written in "dictionary" order.

This can cause a problem for package development on Debian because of the following scenario:

Upstream creates a distribution tarball for PyPI using `python3 setup.py sdist`.  Upstream includes a setup.cfg that does not have an [egg_info] section.  The resulting tarball will have an [egg_info] section added, containing tag_svn_revision, tag_build, and tag_date keys.

Using Debian build tools may also run the egg_info command which will rewrite the [egg_info] stanza, but it may do so with the keys *in a different order*.  This will cause the build tools to see an unaccounted for change in the upstream file and will cause the source package build process to fail.  Because there order is unpredictable, there's no good way to ensure that the build process will produce the same setup.cfg file that the sdist command generated.

The egg_info command, whether run explicitly or implicitly as part of sdist, *must* write that stanza in a predictable order.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/issue/280

issue_title: UTF-8 character incorrectly interpreted as OSC
issue_body:
When trying to upgrade my project to use the current git master, I needed to send data as bytes.
This works, however I can't decode UTF-8 data. For example, the following test script will fail:
```
import pyte
screen = pyte.Screen(10, 1)
stream = pyte.Stream(screen)
c = b"\xE2\x80\x9D" #u+201d
stream.feed(c)
print(screen.display)
```
The first character of the screen should be '”', but is blank.

It looks like pyte is interpreting the 9d as OSC, causing problems from there.
According to Wikipedia articles on [ANSI escape codes][1] and [C0 and C1 control codes][2], in UTF-8 mode, OSC (also ST, CSI) should be encoded in UTF-8, so in this case 0xc2 0x9d.

[1]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#Sequence_elements
[2]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C0_and_C1_control_codes

issue_title: Help reproducing fitparams task
issue_body:
Disclaimer: _This is not an issue with `photutils`. I'm just out of ideas and I don't where else to turn for help. I will therefore understand completely if this is closed on the spot. That said, I will really appreciate any help you could give me, since I've been trying to figure this out for weeks now._

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
I'm trying to reproduce the [fitparams](http://stsdas.stsci.edu/cgi-bin/gethelp.cgi?fitparams) task from the `photcal` package.

I've already sent en email to the STScI (the developers of Pyraf), posted in the [IRAF forum](http://iraf.net/forum/viewtopic.php?msg=1&showtopic=1469632), and even wrote to Lindsey Davis. The STScI guys told me they don't work with this package and I should ask in the forum, the forum post has been sitting there for ~20 days (it doesn't look like it's going to receive an answer), and I never got a response from Dr Davis.

In page 32 of the [A user's guide to stellar CCD photometry with IRAF](https://www.mn.uio.no/astro/english/services/it/help/visualization/iraf/daophot2.pdf)  guide, an output of doing a `:vshow` in `fitparams` is shown in Fig 27:

![captura realizada el 2017-05-23 16 36 17](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6054951/26372927/fd75dfe0-3fd5-11e7-9cda-03c915157da2.png)

Where `function, fit` are the observed and predicted values in the best found least squares regression, `residuals=function-fit`, and I have no idea how `sigma` is obtained or what it represents (issue 1. below). I'd like to reproduce the statistical values found for the fit. I can (almost) reproduce most of these:

```
standard deviation 0.0214969
reduced chi        1.0036573
average error      0.0214577
average scatter    ?
RMS                0.0183326
```


 as follows :

* **standard deviation**

      sum_sq = np.sum((function - fit) ** 2)
      nu = len(function) - deg
      stddev = np.sqrt(sum_sq / nu)

   where `deg=3` since 3 parameters parameters are being fitted.

* **reduced chi**

      chisq = np.sum(((function - fit) / sigma) ** 2)
      nu = len(function) - deg
      red_chi = chisq / nu

* **average error**

      avrg_error = stddev / np.sqrt(red_chi)


* **average scatter**

      avrg_scatter = ?

* **RMS**

      RMS = np.sqrt(((function - fit) ** 2).mean())

The issues I find are:


1. I don't know how the `sigma` column (rightmost column in Fig 27) is obtained. Following the trace, I see that the `iraf/pkg/xtools/inlfit/ingresults.gx` file writes to that column the following values:

        (sqrt (PIXEL (1.0) / Mem$t[wts1+i-1]))

   I assume `Mem$t[wts1+i-1]` is some kind of standard deviation? I tried diving in deeper into the code, but the language used (some kind of mixture between `C` and `Fortran`?) looks like an impossible mess to decipher to me.
1. I can not reproduce the `average scatter` value. I have no idea what formula is used. I've tried several to no avail.
1. Most of the statistical parameters shown above (except the `average scatter`) are *almost* reproduced using those equations. There are visible differences nonetheless,  which I can only assign to floating point rounding errors by `fitparams` (as long as the formulas shown above are correct).

issue_title: Problem with uploading binary files with Python 3
issue_body:
With Python 3, uploading a binary file will fail:

```python
with open('/path/to/a/file.jpg', 'r') as fp:
    file_upload = stripe.FileUpload.create(
        purpose='dispute_evidence',
        file=fp
    )
```
fails with:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  <snip>
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/lib/python3.5/site-packages/stripe/multipart_data_generator.py", line 28, in add_params
    self._write_file(value)
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/lib/python3.5/site-packages/stripe/multipart_data_generator.py", line 54, in _write_file
    file_contents = f.read(self.chunk_size)
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/lib/python3.5/codecs.py", line 321, in decode
    (result, consumed) = self._buffer_decode(data, self.errors, final)
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x89 in position 0: invalid start byte
```

Opening the file as binary (e.g. replacing `'r'` by `'rb'` yields a different issue:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  <snip>
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/lib/python3.5/site-packages/stripe/multipart_data_generator.py", line 28, in add_params
    self._write_file(value)
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/lib/python3.5/site-packages/stripe/multipart_data_generator.py", line 57, in _write_file
    self._write(file_contents)
  File "/Users/ob/.pyenv/versions/3.5.1/lib/python3.5/site-packages/stripe/multipart_data_generator.py", line 50, in _write
    self.data.write(bytes(value, 'utf-8'))
TypeError: encoding or errors without a string argument
```

I think we ought to:

1. Update the Python file upload examples to use `mode='rb'` rather than `mode='r'` (since image files are binary files and it makes no sense to apply an encoding to them).

2. Patch [this method](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/master/stripe/multipart_data_generator.py#L46-L50) that assumes that Python 3 will always deal with Unicode strings. Something like this should work:
```python
def _write(self, value):
  if sys.version_info < (3, 0) or isinstance(value, bytes):
    self.data.write(value)
  else:
    self.data.write(bytes(value, 'utf-8'))
```

@brandur, any thoughts? Should I proceed with the above?

issue_title: Custodian Monitor Not Working with terminate_on_nonzero_returncode=True
issue_body:
## System

* Custodian version: master branch, revision _af15bda_ and _5f0e8b_
* Python version: 3.5.2
* OS version: Cray XC40

## Summary

This test compares two revisions in the master branch: 1) "_5f0e8b9_" which is after adding the return code exception raising code, 2) "_af15bda_" which before adding return code logic. The "run_vasp" is command is used to perform the test. No workflows messed up with the test, only a simple SLURM script and custodian package were involved.

The most important message is from "run.log" file.

1) With revision "_5f0e8b9_":

After "{'errors': ['eddrmm']...", the custodian says "Job return code is 137. Terminating...".

2) With revision "_af15bda_":

After "After "{'errors': ['eddrmm']...", the custodian says "Starting job no. 1 (VaspJob) attempt no. 2". There are other issues which are specific to my environment and are already solved, which I don't think will affect the conclusions. The bottom line is that with the old code is trying to fix the error while the new code exits prematurely.

## Example code

```
module load vasp/5.4.1
run_vasp -c "srun -n 32 vasp_std" static
```

## Error message

```
"Job return code is 137. Terminating..." vs "Starting job no. 1 (VaspJob) attempt no. 2".
```

## Suggested solution (if known)

* When both Monitors and terminate_on_nonzero_returncode=True are requested, disable either of them.
* Document that the Monitor won't work if terminate_on_nonzero_returncode is set to true, otherwise, the users will have the illusion that they still have Custodian Monitor to fix VASP errors.

null

issue_title: templates: custom Jinja template checker
issue_body:
In Invenio 1, when a site customised their Python templates, the admins could use:

```console
$  python templates.py --check-custom-templates
```

to make sure that their local customisations are still OK after the upstream Invenio code was updated.
This was part of `make check-upgrade` procedures.

In Invenio 2, we don't have equivalent functionality yet.  It would be good to write a tool that would attempt to parse Jinja templates and compare whether the overlay customisations are coherent with Invenio base templates, and warn the user if they are not.

issue_title: ReportLab 3.4.0 broke lookaheads (the stuff required for "Page X of Y")
issue_body:
ReportLab 3.4.0 has some paragraph width fixes but it also added some "caching" which checks if the width of a paragraph fragment has been calculated already. If the width has been calculated then the contents of the fragment aren't recalculated. See: https://bitbucket.org/rptlab/reportlab/diff/src/reportlab/platypus/paragraph.py?diff2=7c65c6e52b13&at=default#Lsrc/reportlab/platypus/paragraph.pyT449

This broke how I implemented `getName` which need to be reevaluated on the second build pass.

I can't see a good way to fix this. Resetting the `frags` attribute on each pass leads to weird problems so either I'm missing some fragments or more stuff has been cached. Keeping a clean copy of the flowables results in the second pass being appended to the end of the document instead of overwriting the first pass.

I think the only solution is to stop using ReportLab's built in `multiBuild` and refactor z3c.rml to create two separate lists of flowables and build each one individually but share the manager. Only real problem with this is if you have lookaheads and use indexes it will require four passes instead of two. Maybe there's a simpler way but good luck understanding ReportLab's source.

issue_title: Service notification launch intent causes app crash
issue_body:

If the user clicks the notification they get "Stopped working" dialog, but the app does launch.

Build spec: https://github.com/freshollie/cashpassport-tracker/blob/master/buildozer.spec

# (list) List of service to declare
#services = NAME:ENTRYPOINT_TO_PY,NAME2:ENTRYPOINT2_TO_PY

def start_tracker_service(self):

        mActivity = autoclass('org.kivy.android.PythonActivity').mActivity

        trackerService.start(mActivity, argument)

issue_title: automated build failure with devpi-client
issue_body:
The name of the package is not returned properly with 

`$ python setup.py --name
`

Adding the following to setup.py allows it work:

```
$ diff -u setup.py~ setup.py
--- setup.py~	2016-04-11 15:37:27.392604445 -0700
+++ setup.py	2016-04-11 15:35:54.493381566 -0700
@@ -43,7 +43,7 @@
                       setup_requires=requirements
     )
     if (len(sys.argv) >= 2 and ('--help' in sys.argv[1:] or sys.argv[1]
-    in ('--help-commands', 'egg_info', '--version', 'clean'))):
+    in ('--help-commands', 'egg_info', '--version', 'clean', '--name'))):
 
         # For these actions, NumPy is not required.
         #



```

issue_title: Invalid version number with pre-release NumPy
issue_body: Numexpr uses distutils' `StrictVersion` which gives an "invalid version number" error with a development version of NumPy.

issue_title: Release 0.7.0
issue_body: The recent re-addition of Yahoo! will trigger a new release in the next week.  I will try to get as many PRs in before then.

issue_title: method test_get_quote_string is already defined
issue_body:
Hello,

in `test_data.py`, method `test_get_quote_string` is defined twice.

issue_title: Make `test_run_details` more useful
issue_body:
`test_run_details` is now almost useless for small tests with over half of it being the adb command logs.

This is misleading users to use their own logger and doing `try:...except Exception:...` to get stacktrace since they get the initial impression that `test_run_details` is too spammy.

While it is important to log adb cmds, we should try to make it less spammy. I don't know what the right answer is. Here are some solutions I could think of:
 - Log adb cmds to a different file
 - Have a clearer prefix for adb cmd log lines.
 - Have an easy way to filter adb cmds out of `test_run_details`.

issue_title: Potential "Control flow issues" detected by Coverity
issue_body:
Updating to [brotli 1.0.5 in Firefox](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1474122), coverity discovered two new issues (CID 1437666 & 1437667)

For 
https://github.com/google/brotli/blob/master/c/enc/hash_rolling_inc.h#L131
Coverity says: 

> dead_error_condition: The condition (position & 0UL) != 0UL cannot be true.

And for 
https://github.com/google/brotli/blob/master/c/enc/hash_rolling_inc.h#L164

> dead_error_condition: The condition (cur_ix & 0UL) != 0UL cannot be true.

As they do not seem to be false positive, I prefer to report them!

issue_title: The full alphabet is not preserved and the transformation is not reversible.
issue_body:
Hi,
Thank you for publishing such a great research!

After studying your docs we assumed that the default settings for training will retain the full alphabet so that the transformation is reversible.   Based on that assumption we build our model for polish NLP contest and we are now trying not to get disqualified :(, (I hope that the competition jury will reason with us.)

We tracked the issue down to the default setting of character_coverage which is set to 0.9995. 
I understand that normally this is recommended setting as the model may work better like that. But it would be good to add a mention of that setting in the docs so to help anyone that needs to have the transformation reversible.

I can submit PR to emphasize the fact that the default `character_coverage` is not 1.0 but I'm not sure how this parameter works.

For anyone that comes across this issue. Just train your model with `---character_coverage 1.0`.
In our case, the default coverage of 0.9995  removed as many as 4 characters from our alphabet of size 92. The characters were indeed least frequently used so I guess that the coverage means how much of the text is going to be restored after reversing the transformation but that just guessing.

Anyway, this is a super small issue, and the algorithm is very useful. Once again thank you for making the library public.

Regards,
Piotr

issue_title: RGDA1 graph canonicalization sometimes still collapses distinct BNodes
issue_body:
During the [evaluation of my graph pattern learner](https://github.com/RDFLib/graph-pattern-learner/blob/master/eval.py#L433) i'm currently trying to generate all possible (different) SPARQL BGPs of a given length (5 at the moment). With up to 11 variables, enumerating all of those graphs might be stretching it a bit, but i'm nearly there. However, to do this, i need the canonical representations of SPARQL BGPs. As discussed before (#483), i'm reducing SPARQL BGPs (and especially their variables) to RDF graphs with BNodes (see [here](https://github.com/RDFLib/graph-pattern-learner/blob/master/graph_pattern.py#L189) if interested), then run RGDA1 on it, and map the canonical BNode labels to the SPARQL Variables.

Similarly to #494, I noticed that sometimes during this process i still lose nodes. Minimal test-case below (PR with test will follow):

```python
g = rdflib.Graph()
g += [
 (rdflib.term.BNode('N0a76d42406b84fe4b8029d0a7fa04244'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#object'),
  rdflib.term.BNode('v2')),
 (rdflib.term.BNode('N0a76d42406b84fe4b8029d0a7fa04244'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#predicate'),
  rdflib.term.BNode('v0')),
 (rdflib.term.BNode('N0a76d42406b84fe4b8029d0a7fa04244'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#subject'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'urn:gp_learner:fixed_var:target')),
 (rdflib.term.BNode('N0a76d42406b84fe4b8029d0a7fa04244'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#Statement')),
 (rdflib.term.BNode('N2f62af5936b94a8eb4b1e4bfa8e11d95'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#object'),
  rdflib.term.BNode('v1')),
 (rdflib.term.BNode('N2f62af5936b94a8eb4b1e4bfa8e11d95'),

 (rdflib.term.BNode('N5ae541f93e1d4e5880450b1bdceb6404'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#object'),
  rdflib.term.BNode('v5')),
 (rdflib.term.BNode('N5ae541f93e1d4e5880450b1bdceb6404'),

issue_title: Remove duplicate Frame properties
issue_body:
There are several `Frame` properties that are also present in `core.DataTable` object:
`_nrows`, `_ncols`, `_nkeys`, `_ltypes`, `_stypes`, `_id`. We should remove them from `Frame`, so that they exist only in the internal object. The accessor properties would then return the values from the internal `_dt`. 

This is a prerequisite for #1066

issue_title: Sparkling Water fails to create h2oContext in simple spark project
issue_body:
I am setting up for the first time Sparkling Water on a standalone cluster running spark 2.2. I have run Sparkling Water on such a cluster before via R (using rsparkling + sparklyr + h2o), but am having issues setting this up as a spark application (in scala).

The app is built with Maven, so I have added the latest sparkling water dependancy:

    <dependency>
        <groupId>ai.h2o</groupId>
        <artifactId>sparkling-water-core_2.11</artifactId>
        <version>2.2.2</version>
    </dependency>
Then the app code is as follows:

```
package com.me.app

import org.apache.spark.sql.{DataFrame, SparkSession}        
import org.apache.spark.h2o._
import water.Key
import water.fvec.Frame

object sparklingWaterH2o {

  def sparklingWaterH2o(): Unit = {


    val sparkSession = SparkSession
      .builder()
      .master("spark://clsuter.address:0077")
      .appName("sparklingWaterH2o")
      .config("spark.executor.memory", "32G")
      .config("spark.executor.cores", "5")
      .config("spark.cores.max", "40")
      .config("spark.ext.h2o.nthreads", "40")
      .config("spark.jars", "/path/to/fat/jar/app-1.0-SNAPSHOT-jar-with-dependencies.jar")
      .getOrCreate()

    val h2oContext = H2OContext.getOrCreate(sparkSession)

    import h2oContext._

    val df = Seq(
      (1, "2014/07/31 23:00:01"),
      (1, "2016/12/09 10:12:43")).toDF("id", "date")

    val h2oTrainFrame = h2oContext.asH2OFrame(df)

    println(s"h2oContext = ${h2oContext.toString()}")
```

I then compile the fat jar to send to the cluster, however the h2oContext never gets created and the SparkContext gets shut down with exit code 255. The app exiting with no error codes before an h2o context is created - the only potentially useful message is IP address not found on this machine.

I've tried this with Sparkling Water version 2.2.0 and get the same issues, also tried adding dependencies for sparkling-water-ml and sparkling-water-repl, as well as adding all the h2o core dependencies (though assuming these are not needed as they are integrated into sparkling water?). 

The strange thing is that I get almost the exact same issue when trying to connect via R (using rsparkling and sparklyr, see [here](https://github.com/h2oai/rsparkling/issues/57)) - and that method worked correctly up until a few weeks ago.

issue_title: How to chose eta, crossing over probability and mutation probability
issue_body:
The class `DEAPOptimisation `has parameters, that affect the optimization, most importantly `mutpb`, `cxpb `and `eta`. How do you usually chose these parameters? I see, that they default to `cxpb = 1`, `mutpb = 1`, `eta = 10`. In the example of the L5PC, the default values are (implicitly) used in the toy example of 2 generations.  in gaupnerbrunelstdp, `eta=20, mutpb=0.3, cxpb=0.7` is used. Why? Is there a rule of thumb, which parameters give good results? 

If I use 1000 individuals and 1000 generations, I see, that a feasible solution is only found in ~30% of the optimizations and I am therefore wondering, if I just choose the parameters wrong. Or is this the expected success rate?

issue_title: Font Awesome 5 changed Unicode code points
issue_body:
From reading https://fontawesome.com/how-to-use/upgrading-from-4 I thought I could upgrade easily to Font Awesome 5. Nothing is ever easy.

I have a lot of CSS that adds pretty icons in front of certain styles, like this:

```css
 .annotation:not(.token):before {
    font-family: FontAwesome;
    content: "\F24A\00A0";
 }
```

The upgrade guide didn't tell me I needed to change the `font-family`.

The upgrade guide didn't tell me I need to add a certain font weight. (Why!? Now I have to look up each one of these codes.)

But worst of all, `F24A` doesn't work anymore!

1. I have to go to https://fontawesome.com/v4.7.0/cheatsheet/ to find out what F24A was for. Ah, it was a ` fa-sticky-note-o`.
2. Now I have to go to https://fontawesome.com/how-to-use/upgrading-from-4 and look up `sticky-note-o`, finding out it changed to `sticky-note` in the "regular" group.
3. Then I have to go to https://fontawesome.com/cheatsheet, go to the "regular" section, search for `sticky-note`, and find out that the code is now `F249`.

Why? _Why?_ It's the same icon. Why did the Unicode code point have to change??

Now I'm going to have to tediously go through each one of these encodings, which is very error prone and will take ages. Why, Font Awesome? Why switch Unicode code points around?? Why cause pain?

issue_title: Patroni does not detect extremely stale replicas
issue_body:
In the event a replica is very far behind, several of these errors will appear in the Postgres logs upon starting Patroni:

    FATAL:  could not receive data from WAL stream: ERROR:  requested WAL segment 00000005000000000000001B has already been removed

In the case a replica can not catch up to the primary node, Patroni should stop the replica and either rebuild the node, or quit attempting to rejoin the cluster.

issue_title: swagger_spec_validator adds in x-scope property to definitions
issue_body:
When validating the swagger document the dictionary that is passed to `swagger_spec_validator.validator20` is modified. It is possible for that library to add in any properties it wants to the spec.

Expected behavior, validation doesn't add in extra properties like `x-scope` to references.

Actual behavior, validation adds in extra properties like `x-scope` to references which can cause issues when using swagger clients that rely on the swagger_spec validator (like bravado). If the validator sets `x-scope` to the empty string then the bravado client can't properly understand where those references point to.

This was in a new connexion project, the swagger document had parameters defined as references.  


### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
`Python 3.5.1`
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
`Version: 1.0.89`

This is related to this issue though allowing only a copy of the spec to be passed into the validator is a good idea on this side as well. https://github.com/Yelp/swagger_spec_validator/pull/48

issue_title: hbmqtt_sub is missing option `-v` (verbose) to print topic names
issue_body:
The command `hbmqtt_sub` seem to closely resemble `mosquitto_sub` command.

Anyway, the `hbmqtt_sub` is missing an option `-v` for verbose output, which is prepending topic name and a space (or tab) before printing message content.

This feature (having topic prepended) allows scriptping, where `cut` command can split the topic name, perform some scripting etc.

Consider adding the `-v` option.

issue_title: Définition du revenu disponible
issue_body:
Bonjour @cbenz,
J'ai effectué ces derniers jours des simulations de revenus de base financés par la csg imposable et je me suis rendu compte que la variable "revenu_disponible" ne prend en compte la "csg_imposable_salaire" que jusqu'à un montant de salaire brut variable selon la configuration familiale (environ 3300 euros de salaire brut mensuel pour une personne seule sans enfant et environ 6700 euro pour un couple avec 3 enfants), selon une logique que je ne comprends pas. Par contre, au-delà de ce montant, la csg imposable n'est plus du tout prise en compte. Il semble donc que le calcul du revenu disponible se fasse actuellement sur la base de revenus (et retraites) imposables et non sur celle de revenus (et retraites) nets, ceux-ci étant calculés après déduction de la csg imposable (et de la crds).
Je tiens à préciser que, dans mes simulations, je supprime la csg déductible, mais cette option est sans effet sur l'anomalie constatée pour la csg imposable. 
J'ai donc modifié la classe "revenu_disponible" dans mon notebook, en prenant en compte pour son calcul  "csg_imposable_salaire",ainsi que "csg_imposable_retraite" et "crds" qui ne sont pas non plus prises en compte. Et cela fonctionne. Mes tests sont concluants.
Si je finance la réforme non par la csg imposable, mais par la csg déductible, et si je supprime la déductibilité de ce prélèvement, le problème est identique, et je dois intégrer la prise en compte de "csg_deductible_salaire" dans le calcul de "revenu_disponible", ce qui semble logique, puisque la csg déductible a alors le même effet que la csg imposable.
A l'occasion, je me pose la question de savoir s'il est bien normal que "revenu_disponible" ne prenne pas non plus en compte les allocations chômage perçues ni la "csg_imposable_chomage".
Si mon constat est correct, ne serait-il pas pertinent pour vous de modifier en conséquence la variable "revenu_disponible" dans openfisca_france ?
Merci d'avance et bonne journée.

issue_title: Write new GO enrichment process
issue_body:
New process slug: go-enrichment. 

Inputs:
* ontology (data:ontology:obo)
* gene_ids (list:basic:string)
* source (basic:string)
* gaf (data:gaf, optional)
* pvalue_threshold (basic:decimal, default=0.1)
* genes_in_term_threshold (basic:integer, default=1)

Algorithm

You will have to use resdk to access GAF data object and map genes.

If gaf input not given:
    If source input is equal to the source of any GAF data object on the platform (query data resources). Download the GAF file of the latest data:gaf object (use resdk). RUN GOTEA    
    If the source input has to be mapped:
        Find out to which of the GAF sources do the gene ids map (by query). Map them, then RUN GOTEA.

If gaf input given:
    If gaf source is equal to source input: 
        RUN GOTEA
    If gaf source is not equal to source input:
        Try to map gene_ids input to gaf source. 
            If nothing mapps raise error “Input genes did not map to GAF gene ids.” 
            If mappings successful: RUN GOTEA

How should we implement the mappings, do queries? I suggest Domen works on the mapping part of the process.

issue_title: Convert CIDs to Unicode?
issue_body:
PDFMiner gives us a bunch of [CID](https://www.adobe.com/content/dam/acom/en/devnet/font/pdfs/5014.CIDFont_Spec.pdf) characters in our output. (e.g. `25(cid:176) C` instead of `25° C`). It would be great to be able to convert these to their respective unicode characters before outputting. Some potentially useful references [1], [2].

[1] https://stackoverflow.com/questions/24089245/decode-cid-font-codes-to-equivalent-ascii-characters
[2] https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/cmap-resources/

issue_body:
Hello, 

I have been using pyart to read in and dealias both NEXRAD Level 2 and CSU-CHILL radar velocity data. I have been using the pyart.correct.dealias_fourdd function. It works great for the NEXRAD data, but the code stalls for the CSU-CHILL data. The CSU-CHILL data I am using is not in the traditional CHL format, but has been converted to uf files (these files are ~30 MB). 

From my own tests, I believe the code is stalling at the following line:
_rsl_interface._label_volume(rsl_volume, radar)

One difference between the data is that the CSU-CHILL data is sector scans, while the NEXRAD data is full sector PPIs. 

Do you have any ideas what may be causing the code to stall with the CSU-CHILL uf files?

I have included below the lines that read in the data, as well as the call to the dealias_fourdd.

file = '/Volumes/42/CHILL/ORIG/20160717/CHL20160717_222347.uf'
radar = pyart.io.read_uf(file,field_names={'DZ':'DBZ', 'VE':'VEL'})
dealias=pyart.correct.dealias_fourdd(radar,sounding_heights=aint, sounding_wind_speeds=wsint,\
                                      sounding_wind_direction=wdint,vel_field='VEL')

Please note that I have tried several different read commands (pyart.io.read_uf, pyart.io.read, not including the field_names specification) with the same code stall. Also, I use the same sounding data for the NEXRAD and CHILL data, and since it works with the NEXRAD data, I am assuming that is not the issue for the CHILL data.

Thanks for your time,

Peter

issue_title: A Compliment and Quandary
issue_body:
A co-worker of mine showed me this library. I think this is an excellent use of bitmaps! I think I see another interesting extension of this bitmapist library and I wanted to get your feedback. 

I work for a company that needs an almost real-time recommendation engine. I started thinking about the application of bitmaps into [collaborative filtering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collaborative_filtering). Basically in collaborative filtering you need a matrix that is comprised of `[users * products]`

![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/52/Collaborative_filtering.gif)

The idea in this instance would basically be to have a `SETBIT users:20160614:1 [product_id] 1` for each user that is representative of what product they like. You would also need to have a `SETBIT products:20160614:1 [user_id] 1` this would have an index into each user.  

Wait I think I see a problem with this. How would we represent an empty space in the matrix? A cooccurrence matrix has 3 states (Like, Dislike, Unknown). I guess you could probably combine them in some way or maybe my bit operations are rusty. 

The main benefits in this would be the storage savings that this could have. When I did a quick calculation based on traffic a site like ours would see in a day it would be about (700 products * 300k users) 26.25MB

I understand that you are probably not interested in having this be part of this repo. I'd mainly like your feedback and advice. Thanks!

issue_title: Spanner library should have a way to track leaked sessions
issue_body: Due to a bug in user code or client library code, it is possible that a session is checked out from the pool but never released. We should make it easier for users to be able to detect such leaks and debug where in there code the leak occurred. One way can be to record the stack trace in a list or a map whenever a session is checked out and remove that trace when the session is released back to the pool. User should be able to look at this list and figure out the location in the code where all the unreleased sessions were checked out at. We can also log these stack traces when the pool is closed.

issue_title: Bigtable: how to simplify the retry pattern
issue_body:
For PR #4662, we want to find a concise way of retrying any gRPC request, based on the receipt of a previous exception.  This was done for row.commit() by creating a RetryCommit class to 1) retain state information for retrying the request and 2) provide a `__call__()` method which will actually retry the request:
```
class RetryCommit:
    def __init__(self, table, request_pb):
        """
        :param table:
        :param request_pb:
        """
        self.table = table
        self.request_pb = request_pb

    def __call__(self):
        """
        :return: raise exception for MutateRow
        """
        client = self.table._instance._client
        client._data_stub.MutateRow(self.request_pb)
```
In DirectRow.commit(), 1) create the request, 2) create the retry, identifying the exceptions for which to retry, 3) start the retry code (including first request), 4) catch timeouts on the retry, as below:

```
        request_pb = messages_v2_pb2.MutateRowRequest(
            table_name=self._table.name,
            row_key=self._row_key,
            mutations=mutations_list,
        )

        retry_commit = RetryCommit(self._table, request_pb)
        retry_ = retry.Retry(
            predicate=retry.if_exception_type(exceptions.GrpcRendezvous),
            deadline=30)

        try:
            retry_(retry_commit)()
        except exceptions.RetryError:
            raise concurrent.futures.TimeoutError(
                'Operation did not complete within the designated '
                'timeout.')
```

What other ways can retry for a request be expressed to make this more concise?  It  needs to support 1-4 above.  Can more functionality be moved into the Retry? How?

The result should ideally be a pattern which can be used to implement retries for a wide range of gRPC requests.  Note that cloud bigquery client methods all have an optional retry argument of type `google.api_core.retry.Retry`.

issue_title: pageSize vs. maxResults
issue_body:
What is the point of the two arguments? For old-style APIs (i.e. discovery based), JSON paging is done via `maxResults`.

For new-style APIs (i.e. those with gRPC support), paging is done via `pageSize` (both in JSON and gRPC).

Currently our iterator takes `maxResults` to mean a maximum **over many pages** while `pageSize` (at least in terms of the two english words put together) means **the size of one page**.
- Is it worth offering distinct `page_size` and `max_results` keyword params?
- @lukesneeringer, is there an equivalent concept of max results vs. page size in GAX?
- When no `pageSize` is used in a new-style API what happens? Some of our GAX helper docstrings say "If not passed, defaults to a value set by the API."

/cc @bjwatson @tseaver @jonparrott 

Relevant to https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pull/3270#discussion_r110059932

**NOTE**: This is copied mostly verbatim from an old email thread.

issue_title: Update to Language v1
issue_body:
### What
Update the hand-written code to Language v1, which was publicly announced yesterday (11/15). The new GAPIC code is in a [PR](https://github.com/googleapis/api-client-staging/pull/104), which will be published to PyPI as soon as its approved.

### Why
Language v1beta1 has been replaced by v1 as of Tuesday 11/15. It has some additional features we want to support.

This should be done soon, so that we can update the public documentation and samples to reference the `google-cloud-language` library.

issue_title: node change is ignored in ParallelPool
issue_body: See test case added in #63.  Calling `_serve` with a new node setting *should* create a new pool, or at least change the number of nodes.  The former (create a new pool) is the behavior for the other Pools.

issue_title: Create 3.0.stable branch
issue_body: As it says in the title. You've got a 2.0.stable branch and a 2.1.stable branch, and I would like there to be a 3.0.stable branch. My reasons are that I link people to use the `~> 3.0.0` version of `bootstrap-sass` in Rails 4 in Action, and then mention that they can pick-and-choose the assets to import, rather than just doing `@import "bootstrap";`. By being able to link to a 3.0.stable branch, they would have the latest asset lists rather than potentially an asset list from an older version of bootstrap-sass.

issue_title: 给shadowsocks添加中继功能
issue_body:
# 0x00 
所谓中继功能，就是在sslocal 通过一个或者多个中继访问 ssserver，使用场景例如在阿里云上部署一个中继，本地通过阿里云在访问墙外ssserver

# 0x01
中继除了优化路由，提升访问速度以外，更重要的还能更好隐藏ip，比如你本地连墙外ip，到微博发了一个贴，结果还是被查到了ip，这个是因为墙可以保存进出配对记录。首先微博查到墙外ip，在通过此刻谁连接了墙外ip，获知你的墙内ip， 如果墙外做一次中继则不会发生。

# 0x02
shadowsocks原本做中继的方式是通过iptables 或者第三方转发软件，这里我给shadowsocks的local.py添加一个-r 功能，表明它是一个中继节点，它原封不动的把下游请求，转发到上游，上游可以是一个ssserver，或者另一个中继，上下游的参数设置方式和原本的一样
````
python local.py -b 0.0.0.0 -l localport -s your_server -p your_server_port -k your_password -r
````

# 0x03
代码位置 https://github.com/wobushizhanghua/shadowsocks  develop分支 欢迎访问

issue_title: bdist_pex.run() doesn't resolve cache-dir's "{pex_root"}
issue_body:
bdist_pex.run() doesn't rewrite the {pex_root} token as the "pex" cmdline frontend does which leads
to a directory named "{pex_root}/" in the root-folder of the project.

Adding the same rewrite to pex/commands/bdist_pex.run() fixes it:

    if options.cache_dir:
      options.cache_dir = make_relative_to_root(options.cache_dir)

Introduced by #206

issue_title: pex throws a resolution error when using cache, succeeds otherwise
issue_body:
when passing two requirements of the form (unpinned, pinned) for the same package (e.g. 'thrift thrift==0.9.1'), pex has different behavior in resolution depending on whether or not the cache is used. this also manifests in consumers of pex (i.e. pants) when dependencies land in a similar order.

repro:

```
[illuminati build]$ pex --version
pex 1.0.3
[illuminati build]$ rm -rf ~/.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex thrift thrift==0.9.1 -o test.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex thrift thrift==0.9.1 -o test.pex
Could not satisfy all requirements for thrift:
    thrift, thrift==0.9.1
```

this also repros under 1.1.0 and for different packages:

```
[illuminati build]$ pex --version
pex 1.1.0
[illuminati build]$ rm -rf ~/.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex psutil psutil==3.2.1 -o test.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex psutil psutil==3.2.1 -o test.pex
Could not satisfy all requirements for psutil:
    psutil, psutil==3.2.1
```

and is also affected by ordering:
```
[illuminati build]$ rm -rf ~/.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex psutil==3.2.1 psutil -o test.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex psutil==3.2.1 psutil -o test.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex psutil==3.2.1 psutil -o test.pex
[illuminati build]$ pex psutil psutil==3.2.1 -o test.pex
Could not satisfy all requirements for psutil:
    psutil, psutil==3.2.1
```
