issue_title: Default values for webargs
issue_body:
Now that webargs is using marshmallow internally, we specify default argument values using the `missing` parameter:

```
arg = fields.Str(missing='foo')
```

But apispec doesn't check the `missing` key when introspective parameter defaults--it only checks `default`. To get the correct swagger default, we have to specify both parameters:

```
arg = fields.Str(missing='foo', default='foo')
```

Which isn't ideal.

Since we're using the same logic to introspect fields and schemas that are used for serialization and deserialization, one approach would be to tell `field2property` and related methods whether they're introspecting load or dump. For the specific case of parameter defaults, we'd check `default` for dump and `missing` for load.

issue_title: Validating fields across schemas with Schema.validator
required=False, allow_none=True, default=None not supported
issue_body:
Its quite useful to be able to load objects with attributes who's names are present, but who's value is null.

AFAICT, marshmallow's Field class advertises that this should be possible, but this only works for Float and Int fields:

```
    def test_missing_marshmallow_field(self):
        broken_when_missing = []
        not_broken_when_missing = []
        for field in [
            ma.fields.Url(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.Str(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.DateTime(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.Time(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.Dict(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.List(ma.fields.Str(), required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.Float(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.Boolean(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.Int(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
            ma.fields.Field(required=False, allow_none=True, default=None),
        ]:
            try:
                 field._deserialize(None, {}, None)
            except ma.fields.ValidationError as e:
                print field.__class__, e.messages
                broken_when_missing.append(field.__class__)
            else:
                not_broken_when_missing.append(field.__class__)


        print "Broken"
        print not_broken_when_missing
        print "Not Broken"
        print broken_when_missing

        if broken_when_missing:
            raise Exception("Broken when missing: {}".format(broken_when_missing))
```

... fails for all fields except Int and Float

At an absolute minimum these things will need to be done:

- [ ] Ensure everything builds with 3.7 and that the built package can be imported.
- [ ] All unit test pass on both macOS and Linux.
- [ ] Upload 3.7 wheel to PyPI on next release.

We'll probably want to have only beta support for 3.7 in its first release.

issue_title: Error with default value for UUIDField primary key
issue_body:
I'm getting an error with primary keys having a default value.
Let's consider a model with an `UUIDField` (new in django 1.8)
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/models/fields/#uuidfield
```python
import uuid
from django.db import models
class MyModel(models.Model)
    id = models.UUIDField(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4)
```
Docs suggest to set a `default=uuid.uuid4` to automatically generate a value for the field on object creation.
When we try to perform an update (i.e. PUT), a new empty instance of `MyModel` is created in `tastypie.resources.Resource.build_bundle` and, having the field a `default`, it is assigned a new `id`.
Therefore the update fails, since the newly created instance has a different `id` from the one of the object to update.

issue_title: Explicitly call sc.stop() at the end of example code
issue_body: In cluster settings if you don't explicitly call sc.stop() your application may hang. Like closing files, network connections, etc, when you're done with them, it's a good idea to call sc.stop(), which lets the spark master know that your application is finished consuming resources.

issue_title: BigDL optimization throw exception on Spark 2.2.0
issue_body:
Here's the error stack

```
Py4JJavaError: An error occurred while calling o133.optimize.
: org.apache.spark.SparkException: Job aborted due to stage failure: Task 0 in stage 9.0 failed 4 times, most recent failure: Lost task 0.3 in stage 9.0 (TID 35, bigdl-w-1.c.internal, executor 2): java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException: java.lang.NoSuchMethodError: org.apache.spark.storage.BlockInfoManager.unlock(Lorg/apache/spark/storage/BlockId;)V
	at java.util.concurrent.FutureTask.report(FutureTask.java:122)
	at java.util.concurrent.FutureTask.get(FutureTask.java:192)
```

Looks like we need to wrap the BlockManager.unlock method with reflection.

Why is this not caught in the unit test?

We also need to set a verify job for spark 2.2 in our Jenkins job configuration.

Currently, the work around of this issue is to build with ```-Dspark.version=2.2.0```

issue_title: Time zones / Time references
issue_body:
I think we should make sure, that time zones, other time references are used correctly. 
We can have time stamps given as UTC, really related to a time zone, local time (without DST), mean solar time, true solar time, ...? This is an everyday problem from my experience, so we should be able to handle all of these formats at least in the future. 
For now it could be ok to define, that all times should be given as UTC and leave all time conversion issue to the user.

As pandas has [support for Julian dates since 0.14](http://pandas-docs.github.io/pandas-docs-travis/whatsnew.html#whatsnew-0140-enhancements) ([this](https://github.com/pydata/pandas/pull/4041) is the pull request). I was thinking about creating a pull request to implement `to_solar_time` in pandas. However this is not easy to implement and I am not sure, if they accept it. But this way all time conversion would be in pandas an we could implement thinks based on pandas.

issue_title: Are the docs incorrect for including related data?
issue_body:
Hello,

Coming back to this package after using `ember-django-adapter` for awhile and while trying to figure out how to include side-loaded related data, I notice that the docs say it is handled automatically. However in practice out-of-the-box this package does not include the `included` key with the related data unless I explicitly declare `JSONAPIMeta` like so:
```
class UserSerializer(ModelSerializer):
    account = AccountSerializer(read_only=True)

    class JSONAPIMeta:
        included_resources = ['account']
```
Is this by design or is something not hooked up right? For nested/related serializers should the docs explain that you must include the `included_resources` property? I was only able to find this via stackoverflow and another issue on here.

[This](http://django-rest-framework-json-api.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html#extract-included) page is the only page in the docs that even references the property `included_resources` but does nothing to explain how to use it.

Thanks

issue_title: Berry doesn't work for Frankfurt located S3 credential bucket
issue_body:
Got following error for Frankfurt:
```
taupage-init: INFO: Waiting for berry to download OAuth credentials to /meta/credentials/user.json..
berry: ERROR: Could not read from mint S3 bucket "zalando-stups-mint-607300429032-eu-central-1": 400 Bad Request: The authorization mechanism you have provided is not supported. Please use AWS4-HMAC-SHA256.
```

> AWS4-HMAC-SHA256, also known as "V4," is one of two authentication schemes in S3.
>
US-Standard, and many -- but not all -- other regions, also support the other, older scheme, V2
Since Frankfurt was introduced late in 2014, it apparently does not support V2, which is what this error suggests you are using.
>
http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingAWSSDK.html explains how to enable V4 in the various SDKs, assuming you are using an SDK that has that capability.

issue_title: Pierone login accepts any URL
issue_body:
Would be nice to validate if the pierone URL is actually valid. Maybe pinging the address to see if it works and showing an error. The current behaviour leads to user that committed a typo in the pierone URL to think they are logged-in and getting error from `docker push` later with a not very helpful message.

### Current behaviour
Example of what currently happens:
```
$ pierone login --url registry.does-not-exist.example.com
Getting OAuth2 token "pierone".. OK
Storing Docker client configuration in /home/master/.docker/config.json.. OK
```

Then trying to push image using docker cli:
```
$ docker push pierone.stups.zalan.do/bus/hello:b17
The push refers to a repository [pierone.stups.zalan.do/bus/hello]
9c445b8a75e0: Preparing
8a48ff634f1d: Preparing
...
19429b698a22: Waiting
9436069b92a3: Waiting
no basic auth credentials
```
This leads users to think there is a problem with Pierone registry or with Docker which is misleading.

### Suggested behaviour
When trying to login in pierone with a URL of non-pierone server:
```
$ pierone login --url registry.does-not-exist.example.com
ERROR: Not found a valid Pierone registry at registry.does-not-exist.example.com
```

issue_title: Wrong optimization result
issue_body:
For integral `x`,
optimizing for `x` in `x <= 1 + 4 * ((x + 2) / 4)`
produces a model `x = 1`. Yet adding a constraint `x  > 1` gives a new model `x = 4`,
so clearly the initial model is not at an optimum.

issue_title: Move this repository to an organization?
issue_body:
I created this repository on my personal GitHub account, but since this is a joint project, maybe it's better to create an organization and move this repository to it. Added benefits would be more granular access control (commit rights etc.).

Also I would just be able to submit pull requests like everyone else, instead of committing to the repo directly. Currently this is convenient because a lot of the 'base' library is still missing, but I probably don't want to make a habit out of this in the long term.

What do you guys think?

If we decide to do this, it would be a good time to change the name as well (if we're going to do that, see #3), so we only have to update configurations once.

issue_title: Definitions returned from Reaching Definitions
issue_body:
Given the very simple program:
```
int main()
{
	__asm {
		mov ebx, 4
		mov eax, 4
		cmp eax, ebx
		je lbl_6
		mov ecx, 7
		jmp lbl_7
		lbl_6 :
		mov ecx, 8
		lbl_7 :
		push ecx
		call malloc
		add esp, 4
	}
}
```

Which yields the following graph: 
![idacapture](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23474424/43131453-be212b9e-8f41-11e8-8ffc-3000f07a6e2e.PNG)


I run the following code in Angr:
```
import angr

p = angr.Project(r"\path\to\file", load_options={"auto_load_libs": False})
cfg = p.analyses.CFGFast()
func = cfg.functions.function(addr = 0x401000)
# The malloc statement 
observation_points = [(0x40101f , 1)]
result = p.analyses.ReachingDefinitions(func=func, observation_points=observation_points, init_func=True)

print result.one_result.register_definitions.dbg_repr()
```

The result that I get about ecx register is:
`Offset 0xc: [Definition 0x13b6b9e8 {Atom: <Reg 12<4>>, Codeloc: <0x401012 id=0x401012[1]>, Data: DataSet<32>: set([7])}]`

Which means that ECX is defined in 0x401012 which is `mov ecx, 7`. 
However, it completely ignores the branch with: `mov ecx, 8` at 0x401019. 
is it the expected behavior? How did it decide to choose 0x401012 and not 0x401019? And is there a way to ask for all the possible definitions? Everything that I tried to run on always yielded only one definition per register. 
It might be related to the `init_func=True` option, but without it, I'm getting an exception.

issue_title: Refactor reconnection code
issue_body:
It's best to leave reconnection logic to the application, however there are certain cases where it seems better to automatically reconnect/retry.

* Trying to connect to CM, but timing out during `connect()`

Currently considering removing automatic reconnect, except for while trying connect(), and providing a reconnect method that will do exponential backoff. This way the application can then just do:

```python
client.on('disconnect', client.reconnect)

reconnect will essentially be connect with delay. The delay will be reset on successful login.

issue_title: outputSE option for DIRAC jobs
issue_body:
I'm planning to add an ```outputSE``` option to the ```Dirac``` backend for DIRAC jobs in 6.1.20.

This will only become a requirement when a user tries to submit a job that requires outputdata but if a user attempts to submit a job without requesting an outputSE then the job submission should fail.

There is currently no nice automated way to upload data automatically to a writable SE based upon where a user can currently write to.

This will be independent of work on the ```DiracFile``` to make it work in a more vanilla DIRAC friendly way.

Obviously I'll add/change some options for LHCb so that LHCb users are unaffected by this change.

Are there any objections to this?

issue_title: Let gen_remote() require a URL
issue_body:
1. Describe what you expected to happen.

[`pulp_smash.tests.pulp3.file.api_v3.utils.gen_remote`](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/pulp_smash.tests.pulp3.file.api_v3.utils.html#pulp_smash.tests.pulp3.file.api_v3.utils.gen_remote) returns a valid dict for use in creating a remote.

2. Describe what actually happened.

The returned dict is incomplete. A URL must be injected into the returned dict.

3. Describe what version of Pulp and what OS is affected by the issue.

Pulp 3.

4. State steps to re-create the issue.

Execute a call like the following:

```python
client.post(FILE_REMOTE_PATH, gen_remote())  # Boom!
```

This will fail. It would be better if `gen_remote` required a URL:

```python
client.post(FILE_REMOTE_PATH, gen_remote('valid url'))
```

In those cases where one wishes to send a malformed request, the returned dict can be saved:

```python
body = gen_remote('bogus url')
del body['url']
client.post(FILE_REMOTE_PATH, body)  # Boom!
```

5. If there is a related pulp.plan.io issue, link to it, and set the "smash test" field.

6. Choose proper labels. See: [Smash Labels](https://pulp-smash.readthedocs.io/en/latest/about.html#labels)

Done.

issue_title: LC with leading constant dimensions
Support float datatype
Add HTTPS support for requests
Allow errors and data
issue_body:
Sometimes the returned object contains both `errors` and `data`, for instance when executing a query such as:

```graphql
query {
   fieldOk
   fieldFailed
}
```

If `fieldFailed` is null-able and during its resolve it fails, the return will be something like:

```json
{"data": {"fieldOk": "value", "fieldFailed": null},
 "errors": [{"message": "some error message", "paths": ["fieldFailed"]}]
}
```

Then throwing an exception is not the best thing to do. My suggestion is to just pass-thru the result dict and let the user handle that -- but that breaks API, then you may consider a new `execute()` method.

issue_title: InputObjectTypeContainer should set all type attributes
issue_body:
One point made in the 2.0 upgrade guide is this:

> If you are using InputObjectType, you now can access its fields via getattr (my_input.myattr) when resolving, instead of the classic way my_input['myattr'].

However, this is only true if all fields on the input type are _required_.  If the fields are optional, and not provided by the caller, then asking for that value will return a raw graphene field (for example, `List`).

Created #596 to address this.

issue_title: Argument of type `SomeEnum` does not map back to `SomeEnum.VALUE` when used as an argument
issue_body:
I couldn't figure out how to succinctly word the summary.

When receiving an Enum as an argument, it's not possible to compare it against the class that defined it.  That is, `args['episode'] == Episode.NEWHOPE` will be `False` on `query { foo(episode:NEWHOPE) }`.  This seems counter-intuitive

```python
class Episode(Enum):
    NEWHOPE = 4
    EMPIRE = 5
    JEDI = 6


class Query(ObjectType):
    foo = String(
        args={'blah': Episode(default_value=Episode.NEWHOPE)}
    )

    def resolve_foo(self, args, context, info):
        import sys

        blah = args.get('blah')
        return (
            "Value of blah is: {!r}\n"
            "(blah == NEWHOPE) is: {!r}\n"
            "Python version is: {!r}\n"
            ""
        ).format(
            blah,
            blah == Episode.NEWHOPE,
            sys.version
        )

```

```
query {
  foo(blah:NEWHOPE)
}
```

yields (de-JSOnified for easier reading):

```
Value of blah is: 4
(blah == NEWHOPE) is: False
Python version is: '3.5.2 (default, Nov 17 2016, 17:05:23) \\n[GCC 5.4.0 20160609]'
```


"Native" Enums don't compare to their 'actual' representation either...

```python
>>> import enum
>>> class Episode(enum.Enum):
...     NEWHOPE = 4
...
>>> Episode.NEWHOPE == 4
False
```
... but they can be converted; e.g. `Episode(4) == Episode.NEWHOPE`, which does not work in Graphene.   Additionally, Python's IntEnum class does permit straight comparisons to integer values.

The behavior here appears to contradict the documentation (emphasis mine):

> graphene.Enum uses enum.Enum internally (or a backport if that’s not available) *and can be used in the exact same way*.

IMHO, the correct behavior here would be for resolve_foo() to get `Episode.NEWHOPE` rather than `4` for the argument here, but this presumably would break a lot of existing code.  Perhaps some sort of option to accomodate this?  

Alternatively, having a `graphene.IntEnum` that's functionally equivalent to `enum.IntEnum` would be nice.

issue_title: Default resolver does not work for Enums
issue_body:
If no explicit resolver is given, graphene nicely resolves fields by trying to access the attribute of the same name on the current node. This does not work for Enum's though, as illustrated by the following example: 

```
import graphene


class SomeEnum(graphene.Enum):
    Bar = 0
    Baz = 1


class Query(graphene.ObjectType):

    foo = graphene.Field(SomeEnum)
    biz = graphene.Field(graphene.String)


class SomeObject:
    def __init__(self):
        self.foo = SomeEnum.Bar
        self.biz = 'foobar'


if __name__ == '__main__':
    obj = SomeObject()
    schema = graphene.Schema(Query)
    query = '{foo,biz}'
    result = schema.execute(query, root_value=obj)
    print(result.data['foo'])
    print(result.data['biz'])
```

This should print

    Bar
    foobar

is does however print

    None
    foobar

I would have expected this to either return`enum.value` or raise an exception, instead of silently returning `None`.

The obvious solution is to add a custom resolver, of course:

    class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
        foo = graphene.Field(SomeEnum)
        biz = graphene.Field(graphene.String)
        
        def resolve_foo(root, *args, **kwargs):
            return root.foo.value

I'd think we should either remove the implicit default-resolver altogether, or take care that all graphql-types are handled properly.

I am transitioning to python from C#, I know in C# the problem of over fetching data has not yet been solved. But in javascript it has been solved by [Join Monster](https://github.com/stems/join-monster).

I have been looking at the issues here, and I see that there is a couple instances of recommendations on how to use lazy loaders to solve the N+1 problem. But I haven't seen any on how to solve the over fetching issue. 

Is there a supported solution for over fetching currently or am I on my own piecing it together from other sources? 

A second question is if Dataloaders is now the recommended method of solving the N+1 problem or are lazy loaders the recommended method?

issue_title: Create a module that exposes all first-party plugin summaries
issue_body:
A user who wishes to use the new-style summaries will currently have to write,
```py
from tensorboard.plugins.histograms import summary as histogram_summary

foo = histogram_summary.op(…)
```
(The import alias is necessary in the common case where the user wants to use summaries of multiple types.)

This is a significant increase in verbosity over the status quo, which only requires
```py
import tensorflow as tf

foo = tf.summary.histogram(…)
```

Per previous discussion, we'd like to keep the main `tensorboard` module a normal module (no `__init__` file) for readability and tooling purposes, but include a module `tensorboard.summary` that imports the summary `op` and `pb` files from individual plugins. The API would be
```py
from tensorboard import summary

foo = summary.histogram(…)
foo_pb = summary.histogram_pb(…)
```

issue_title: We should add 32-bit x86 tests to travis
issue_body:
We've identified many bugs that are invisible when built with 64-bit but are broken on 32-bit. See #200 and #163.

Considering that the vast majority of platforms that we are targeting are either 32-bit or 8-bit, we should consider either making the cert test compile and run everything as 32-bit code or add an additional 32-bit test.

issue_title: Examples should output to unique directory
issue_body: The posix example creates a unique folder in the `output` directory, like `x86_64-apple-darwin` on a mac. The other platforms examples dump their binaries into the `bin` folder, overriding previous builds from potentially different platforms. Can we move to unique platform-specific directories in `output`? Ex. `output/cc2538/bin.` It would be even better to have devkit-specific directories but that might not be needed at this time. Ex. `output/cc2538-cc2538dk` or `output/cc2538-firefly`.

issue_title: Support artman config without REPOROOT
issue_body:
REPOROOT causes confusion when running artman in remote mode -- on the local machine, it corresponds to a directory that contains both the real googleapis repo and a "local_repo", whereas remotely it refers to a single directory named "googleapis" that corresponds to the merged value of both of these.

The artman config refers only to the remote setup; a path such as `{REPOROOT}/googleapis/foo/bar.py` may refer locally either to `googleapis/foo/bar.py` or to `{LOCAL_REPO}/foo/bar.py`. This is particularly confusing because the `googelapis` in the `{REPOROOT}/googleapis/foo/bar.py` may not exist on the local path if the file refered to is actually `{LOCAL_REPO}/foo/bar.py`.

Instead, we could support relative paths in the artman config, like `foo/bar.py`. This does not to reference googleapis, and so solves the issue of having invalid paths in the config on the local machine.

issue_title: Bin model of "Advances in Pre-Training Distributed Word Representations"
issue_body:
I know that this is not exactly and "Issue" and maybe shouldn't be opened here in Github. But still I think that could be interesting for a lot of people and I've posted the same [here ](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/fasttext-library/QGLaGU7hn-U)and received no answers, so:

Hi,

According to this paper [Mikolov, T., Grave, E., Bojanowski, P., Puhrsch, C., & Joulin, A. (2017). Advances in Pre-Training Distributed Word Representations. arXiv preprint arXiv:1712.09405](https://www.google.com/url?q=https%3A%2F%2Farxiv.org%2Fpdf%2F1712.09405.pdf&sa=D&sntz=1&usg=AFQjCNExe_6uHQIED8YdqCXt0hpH4Y_9fA) the main result of their work is the new set of publicly available pre-trained models. 

They state that _"The models trained on the Wikipedia and News corpora, and on the Common Crawl, were published at the fasttext.cc website and are available to the NLP researchers."_

I think they are referring to those available [here under "English word vectors"](https://www.google.com/url?q=https%3A%2F%2Ffasttext.cc%2Fdocs%2Fen%2Fenglish-vectors.html&sa=D&sntz=1&usg=AFQjCNHzfcH3p9B9fCvedrQSMZ7Uyyn1_A) and [here under "Wiki word vectors". 
](https://www.google.com/url?q=https%3A%2F%2Ffasttext.cc%2Fdocs%2Fen%2Fpretrained-vectors.html&sa=D&sntz=1&usg=AFQjCNGxharr2PX3cS6aieOAcobcrV6Blg)
My question is that the ones under "Wiki word vectors" come with the .vec and .bin files but the ones under "English word vectors" come just with the .vec file.

Is it possible to get the .bin file of the pre-trained word vectors that they're talking about in the paper? Or just the .vec file?

Thanks!

issue_title: Should compute explicitly before passing X, y to sklearn scorers
issue_body:
I'm running through @stsievert 's hyperband example.  At one point we pass dask arrays to the score method:

```python
alg = HyperBandCV(...)
...
alg.score(X_test, y_test)
```

This ends up calling an sklearn scorer here:

https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/blob/aa8467964b11f5ee5a89b5dce4dd97c75f541eba/dask_ml/model_selection/_search.py#L806

Scikit-Learn then calls np.asarray separately on X and y, leading to two independent and redundant compute calls.  It would be more efficient to call 

    X, y = dask.compute(X, y)

rather than the implicit

    X = X.compute()
    y = y.compute()

especially when X and y have shared structure.

So we probably need to throw a `dask.compute` call in `DaskBaseSearchCV.score` *if* we suspect that the scorer doesn't support Dask arrays natively.

pybind11 uses deprecated PyThread_set_key_value API
issue_body:
## Issue description

As of Python 3.7, the PyThread_* API was deprecated and superceded by the Thread Specific Storage API.

Details from Python documentation:
* https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/init.html#thread-specific-storage-tss-api
* https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0539/

On Python 3.7, pybind11 should probably switch select the new API.

issue_title: Add more context to "Easy vs Secure" prompt
issue_body:
Right now you get this prompt for redirect

```
Please choose whether HTTPS access is required or optional.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: Easy - Allow both HTTP and HTTPS access to these sites
2: Secure - Make all requests redirect to secure HTTPS access
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

One confusing thing: The title asks "required or optional," but those words don't appear in either option.

Also, we should provide more advice on how to choose. I.e. 

```
1: Easy - Allow both HTTP and HTTPS access to these sites. Choose this if you haven't yet tested that your sites function properly when visited on HTTPS.
2: Secure - Make all requests redirect to secure HTTPS access. Choose this for new sites, or if you're confident your site works on HTTPS. You can undo this change by editing your web server's configuration.
```

issue_title: Improve debug logging of request / response bodies
issue_body:
At debug level `-vv`, we log HTTP request and response bodies. It would be nice to bump up the required log level for this level of detail to `-vvv` so our test runs could be a little quieter.

Also, the printed bodies are escaped and smooshed onto one line. It would be nice to print them literally as they would be seen in HTTP, across multiple lines. This will make it much easier to read the bodies when debugging issues. Right now they are very difficult to use as a diagnostic, and they also clutter up our test logs.

issue_title: Allow non-interactive revocation without deleting certificates
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Xubuntu 17.10

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
git + certbot-auto

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
certbot-auto revoke -n --cert-path /path --key-path /path --reason cessationofoperation

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
There is no option to operate non-interactively AND not delete the certificates. I was able to achieve this prior to https://github.com/certbot/issues/4914 and just got around to updating my scripting to discover this appears to no longer be possible.

This is a request for a flag to NOT delete certs when revoking non-interactively. The reason I need this is so the certificates can still be used to check OCSP and verify that they were successfully revoked, all via non-interactive automation.

issue_title: Wildcard Certificates Support
issue_body:
I have a question regarding the new support in `Let's Encrypt` for wildcard certificates. As [they announced](https://letsencrypt.org/2017/07/06/wildcard-certificates-coming-jan-2018.html).

> ACME v2 and wildcard support will be fully available on February 27, 2018

However, they also say the following.

> Let’s Encrypt will begin issuing wildcard certificates in January of 2018. Wildcard certificates are a commonly requested feature and we understand that there are some use cases where they make HTTPS deployment easier. Our hope is that offering wildcards will help to accelerate the Web’s progress towards 100% HTTPS.

I would like to know if `cerbot` is already is prepared for this, and if so, is there a way to test it?

issue_title: le-auto upgrading fails if virtualenv isn't installed
issue_body: `letsencrypt-auto` fails at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto#L577 if there's a newer version of Certbot while virtualenv isn't installed. This happens because the check for update happens earlier than the check for the existence of virtualenv.

issue_title: How atomic are the updates in storage.py?
issue_body: What will happen if `storage.py` has a crash while trying to `save_successor` or make other changes to a lineage? How safe is the intermediate state going to be?

issue_title: endpoints 2.0.2 - How to generate (and successfully upload) a swagger specification for multiple APIs?
issue_body:
While trying to migrate an existing Endpoints 1.0 GAE Standard project to endpoints framework 2.0, I also ran into trouble trying to figure out how to deploy the multiple Swagger/OpenAPI configuration files for all of the APIs hosted in my application.

Given:

```python
import endpoints
from protorpc import message_types, remote


@endpoints.api(
    name='someapi',
    version='v1',
    allowed_client_ids=[endpoints.API_EXPLORER_CLIENT_ID],
    scopes=[endpoints.EMAIL_SCOPE]
)
class SomeApiService(remote.Service):

    @endpoints.method(
        message_types.VoidMessage,
        message_types.VoidMessage,
        path='someaction',
        http_method='GET',
        name='someaction',
    )
    def someaction(self, request):
        return message_types.VoidMessage()


@endpoints.api(
    name='anotherapi',
    version='v1',
    allowed_client_ids=[endpoints.API_EXPLORER_CLIENT_ID],
    scopes=[endpoints.EMAIL_SCOPE]
)
class AnotherApiV1Service(remote.Service):

    @endpoints.method(
        message_types.VoidMessage,
        message_types.VoidMessage,
        path='anotheraction',
        http_method='GET',
        name='anotheraction',
    )
    def anotheraction(self, request):
        return message_types.VoidMessage()

@endpoints.api(
    name='anotherapi',
    version='v2',
    allowed_client_ids=[endpoints.API_EXPLORER_CLIENT_ID],
    scopes=[endpoints.EMAIL_SCOPE]
)
class AnotherApiV2Service(remote.Service):

    @endpoints.method(
        message_types.VoidMessage,
        message_types.VoidMessage,
        path='anotheraction',
        http_method='GET',
        name='anotheraction',
    )
    def anotheraction(self, request):
        return message_types.VoidMessage()


APPLICATION = endpoints.api_server([SomeApiService, AnotherApiV1Service, AnotherApiV2Service])
```

And then running the following commands:
```
$ python2.7 lib/endpoints/endpointscfg.py get_openapi_spec main.SomeApiService --hostname peerless-rite-129222.appspot.com
No handlers could be found for logger "endpoints.apiserving"
OpenAPI spec written to ./someapiv1openapi.json
$ python2.7 lib/endpoints/endpointscfg.py get_openapi_spec main.AnotherApiV1Service --hostname peerless-rite-129222.appspot.com
No handlers could be found for logger "endpoints.apiserving"
OpenAPI spec written to ./anotherapiv1openapi.json
$ python2.7 lib/endpoints/endpointscfg.py get_openapi_spec main.AnotherApiV2Service --hostname peerless-rite-129222.appspot.com
No handlers could be found for logger "endpoints.apiserving"
OpenAPI spec written to ./anotherapiv2openapi.json
$ CLOUDSDK_CORE_PROJECT=peerless-rite-129222 gcloud service-management deploy *.json
Enabling service endpoints.googleapis.com on project peerless-rite-129222...
Waiting for async operation operations/projectSettings.1712a953-72b6-4b5c-8557-486eda5c9ce7 to complete...
Operation finished successfully. The following command can describe the Operation details:
$ gcloud service-management operations describe operations/projectSettings.1712a953-72b6-4b5c-8557-486eda5c9ce7
ERROR: (gcloud.service-management.deploy) INVALID_ARGUMENT: kind: ERROR
message: "Multiple OpenAPI files cannot have the same \'info.version\' value. Files and Versions found: {anotherapiv1openapi.json:v1, anotherapiv2openapi.json:v2, someapiv1openapi.json:v1}"
```

So it seems like I am not able to deploy Swagger configurations for multiple endpoints framework APIs on the same domain/project/module/service, that have the same version string?

issue_title: Set up tox
Ability to pass request timeouts to Salesforce http operations
issue_body:
In an automated environment, requests to Saleforce not timing can cause really bad service interruptions. I've had cases where a service has "gone silent" for a few days. At the moment most of the API calls have no mechanism to pass in the timeout parameter to `requests`. I've had blocked request when trying to login and when performing operations related to `sobjects`.

Generally we should be able to pass down parameters to `requests`. This issue is also similar to #81

issue_title: TypeError: Can't convert 'bytes' object to str implicitly
issue_body:
When creating a new object via Django Admin which triggers a pop-up interface I ran into this Python3 incompatible implicit type conversion. 
```
Request Method: 	GET
Request URL: 	http://127.0.0.1/admin/example/scene/add/?_to_field=id&_popup=1
Django Version: 	1.10.5
Exception Type: 	TypeError
Exception Value: 	

Can't convert 'bytes' object to str implicitly

Exception Location: 	/opt/example-env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/polymorphic/admin/parentadmin.py in add_type_view, line 362
Python Executable: 	/opt/example-env/bin/python
Python Version: 	3.5.2
````
The problematic line is:
```
extra_qs = ''
if request.META['QUERY_STRING']:
    extra_qs = '&' + request.META['QUERY_STRING']
```

The value of META[QUERY_STRING] is:
```b'_to_field=id&_popup=1'```
Which as you can see is a byte object, and it is being concatinated with the '+' operator which is trigging an invalid implicit conversion in Python 3. The fix I used is to use string substitution...
```
extra_qs = "&%s" % request.META['QUERY_STRING']
```

issue_title: Potential Issues with the same Term being tagged with two "senses"
issue_body:
I was doing some casual testing of similarity and found something that seems to be a bug or at least an inconsistency that could be problematic for people.

It looks like the Entity tagging for the original training set behaves slightly differently than the tagging used for on-the-fly similarity comparisons.  The issue is primarily focused around names of people.  So, for at least a few different people ("Quentin Tarantino", "Dan Harmon", etc) they appear within the `vectors` table tagged as a `PERSON`.  However, when parsing documents on the fly with the model and then looking for similarity, the keys aren't found.  This is because they are being compared using a differently calculated "sense".  They both show up within the target doc tagged as `PROPN`.

So, here you see that `Dan_Harmon|PERSON` is returned as semantically related to the phrase "writers_room" - which is perfectly legit and makes a ton of sense. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17027856/38511773-37e9cbb4-3bf7-11e8-81cb-bde895458f25.png)

 However, if I try to parse the sentence:  "Dan Harmon is one of my favorite writers" or "I really like Dan Harmon as a writer", it yields a Key Error with ...

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17027856/38511797-4ce588b4-3bf7-11e8-96a5-bf8fdd36109b.png)

There are probably a few ways to change this around (maybe by pre-emptively changing out `PROPN` tokens to `PERSON` ? - but that feels like it will almost certainly backfire).  

Let me know your thoughts.

issue_title: Standard stdout and stderr for PBS jobs
issue_body:
When a PBS job failed, it is natural to report stdout/stderr to users. However, the location of these files are usually set by users in the job script and not known to SoS. Perhaps we should

1. Force the use of `.sos/tasks/${task_id}.out` and `.sos/tasks/${task_id}.err`. Basically sos would read from these two files and will report nothing if users specifies anything else in their job file.

2. Make these configurable, namely set something like the following

   ```
    stdout: `${proj_dir}/${task}.out`
    stderr: `${proj_dir}/${task}.err`
    job_template: |
        #PBS -o ${stdout}
        #PBS -e ${stderr}
        #PBS other options
    ```
    so that sos knows what is set by the job template.

issue_title: Style inconsistency makes it hard to contribute using pycharm
issue_body: Hi Jeremy, I know you have particularly strong opinion regarding the code style that suites data science, and I fully support you. Normally I would refrain from starting a discussion about such unimportant topic. However I would like to help document and fix bugs in the library and I've noticed that pycharm cleans up the files automatically to adhere to PEP 8, which makes it hard to use it to make clean commits. To address this I would like to create a style configuration that reflects your conventions for pycharm and for one of the linters (pylint or flake8), and then clean up all inconsistencies such linter would find. I know you use mnemonics in names which make the code shorter, which I find nice to read. I guess that Indent 2 is also chosen to make the code shorter. But how about other parts of [pep8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/). Like whitespace, blank lines (especially after if/else), imports etc? Would you consider adhering to some part of the PEP8 to make it easier for others to contribute and to make it easier to configure the linters? Here is how an example 'resize' function would look like after applying PEP8 advice: How it is now in dataset.py ```py def resize(self, targ, new_path): new_ds = [] dls = [self.trn_dl,self.val_dl,self.fix_dl,self.aug_dl] if self.test_dl: dls += [self.test_dl, self.test_aug_dl] else: dls += [None,None] t = tqdm_notebook(dls) for dl in t: new_ds.append(self.resized(dl, targ, new_path)) t.close() return self.__class__(new_ds[0].path, new_ds, self.bs, self.num_workers, self.classes) ``` After following PEP8 ```py def resize(self, targ, new_path): """Return a copy of ImageData with resized images The images are cached in {self.path}/{new_path}/{targ}/ :param targ: the new size (sz) to resize images to [targ, trag]. :param new_path: the directory where to store resized images relative to self.path :return: a new ImageData that represents directory with resized images """ new_ds = [] dls = [self.trn_dl, self.val_dl, self.fix_dl, self.aug_dl] if self.test_dl: dls += [self.test_dl, self.test_aug_dl] else: dls += [None, None] t = tqdm_notebook(dls) for dl in t: new_ds.append(self.resized(dl, targ, new_path)) t.close() return self.__class__(new_ds[0].path, new_ds, self.bs, self.num_workers, self.classes) ``` Btw there seems to be a bug in the`resize_imgs` used by `ImageData.resize` as the new_path isn't being used. (that for different issue) btw. here are screen showing all stylistic issues pycharm has found:

issue_title: predict method fails for sparse inputs
issue_body:
## Context of the issue

Calling predict with a sparse array raises an exception:
```
TypeError: ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

## Process to reproduce the issue

Run this script:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python
from tpot import TPOTRegressor
import numpy as np
import scipy
lr = TPOTRegressor(config_dict='TPOT sparse', generations=1, population_size=1, random_state=1)
x = scipy.sparse.csr_matrix(np.ones((10, 10)))
y = np.zeros(10)
lr.fit(x, y)
lr.predict(x)
```

## Expected result

No exception

## Current result

A type error is raised.

## Possible fix

Reuse the `_check_dataset` code to check if data passed to `predict` is sparse before trying to impute data.

issue_title: Allow For Configuration of Redirect URI
issue_body:
## Subtitle:  Don't build redirect_uri based on current URL if x is set 

This MIGHT be a no-op, but after a few hours of poking I couldn't find a solution in the current config. I might just be dense about Django internals; if so, I apologize. 

### Scenario
   Nginx proxy/static resource server (docker container: my_proxy)
   Django app in the backend configured (docker app: my_portal)

When a user hits "awesome.example.com/login/github", `my_proxy` receives it and relays it to the `my_portal`. The Django app then redirects over to Github for the authentication flow, but it uses the url name it sees on the HTTP request from the proxy: `http://my_portal/complete/github/redirect_state/...`. 

Obviously Github doesn't handle this well after I authenticate on their end, when it redirects back to my top-level domain,with this value I get a `redirect_uri` error: 

```
Authentication failed: The redirect_uri MUST match the registered callback URL for this application.
```

### Work Around
To work around this I think I can rewriting the headers on the proxy from Nginx to use the top-level domain host that I'm working on (i.e. forwarding `awesome.example.com` to `my_portal` instead of addressing it as `my_portal`). But I believe this prevents (or at least increasingly restricts) me from spinning up multiple Django app backends to serve the application. 

### Ask
Being able to name my application servers whatever internal name I want, but configure the redirection URL would allow me to keep. 

The idea that I think works is allowing the hostname to be overwritten by a setting in Django. I'm bad at naming such things, but `SOCIAL_APP_FQDN` or `REDIRECTION_BASE_URL` would let me configure the plugin to generate `redirect_uri` values that match my app domain, rather than a container host or internal server IP/FQDN. 

### Alternatives
The answer could very well be "this overcomplicates the social stack" or "nobody does it the way you're describing you madperson". If anyone has any guidance or feedback here that I could work into some documentation I would appreciate it!

issue_title: [rllib] Support multiple instances of training within the same process
issue_body:
Currently, due to variable conflicts you cannot instantiate multiple instances of algorithms within the same process. In addition, attempts to save/restore checkpoints will restore variables of all algorithms.

To fix this each algorithm should create its graph within a unique `tf.variable_scope`.

Example: we shouldbe be able to support some workflow like the following:
```
import ray

from ray.rllib.policy_gradient import PolicyGradient, DEFAULT_CONFIG

ray.init()
config = DEFAULT_CONFIG.copy()
config["num_sgd_iter"] = 5
config["timesteps_per_batch"] = 1000
alg1 = PolicyGradient('CartPole-v0', config)
alg2 = PolicyGradient('CartPole-v0', config)

res = alg1.train()
while res.episode_reward_mean < 100:
    res = alg1.train()

alg2.restore(res.checkpoint_path)
while res.episode_reward_mean < 190:
    res = alg2.train()
```

issue_title: Object store running out of memory.
issue_body:
I ran into the following problem today in a friend's experiment (on Ubuntu using Python 2). The code had the structure

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def f():
  return np.zeros(10000000)

results = []
for i in range(100):
  print(i)
  results += ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(50)])
```

On my machine, this script throws an exception. The beginning of the output looks like this.

```
0
1
2
3
[INFO] (/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/src/plasma/eviction_policy.c:178) There is not enough space to create this object, so evicting 0 objects to free up 0 bytes.
Remote function __main__.f failed with:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1602, in process_task
    store_outputs_in_objstore(return_object_ids, outputs, worker)
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1952, in store_outputs_in_objstore
    worker.put_object(objectids[i], outputs[i])
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 441, in put_object
    numbuf.store_list(objectid.id(), self.plasma_client.conn, [value])
numbuf_plasma_out_of_memory.error: The plasma store ran out of memory and could not create this object.


You can inspect errors by running

    ray.error_info()

If this driver is hanging, start a new one with

    ray.init(redis_address="127.0.0.1:25397")
```

Normally, when the object store fills up, it begins evicting objects that are not in use (in a least-recently used fashion). However, because all of the objects are numpy arrays that are being held in the `results` list, they are all still in use, and the memory that those numpy arrays live in is actually in the object store, so they are taking up space in the object store. The object store can't evict them until those objects go out of scope.

We got around this by changing the script to something like the following.

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def f():
  return np.zeros(10000000)

results = []
for i in range(100):
  print(i)
  values = ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(50)])
  values = [np.array(value, copy=True) for value in values]
  results += values
```

This copies the numpy arrays so that the copies backed by the object store memory go out of scope and so the object store can evict those objects.

Of course, the program will eventually run out of memory, but at least the object store will be able to evict stuff. The object store size on Ubuntu was limited to the size of `/dev/shm` which is typically half the amount of memory.

The output of this version on my machine is the following (before the machine runs out of memory).

```
0
1
2
3
[INFO] (/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/src/plasma/eviction_policy.c:178) There is not enough space to create this object, so evicting 35 objects to free up 2800041720 bytes.
4
[INFO] (/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/src/plasma/eviction_policy.c:178) There is not enough space to create this object, so evicting 35 objects to free up 2800041720 bytes.
[INFO] (/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/src/plasma/eviction_policy.c:178) There is not enough space to create this object, so evicting 35 objects to free up 2800041720 bytes.
5
[INFO] (/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/src/plasma/eviction_policy.c:178) There is not enough space to create this object, so evicting 35 objects to free up 2800041720 bytes.
```

issue_title: work flow
issue_body:
fork & PR is a efficient work flow to cooperation, let's try it:

1. fork this repo by click `Fork` button at right top
2. change remote origin to your own repo and remote upstream to this repo:

    ```
    git remote set-url origin YOUR_OWN_REPO_URL
    git remote add upstream git@github.com:opticspy/lightpipes.git
    ```

3. run `git remote -v`, you will see something like this:

    ```
    origin	git@github.com:guyskk/lightpipes.git (fetch)
    origin	git@github.com:guyskk/lightpipes.git (push)
    upstream	git@github.com:opticspy/lightpipes.git (fetch)
    upstream	git@github.com:opticspy/lightpipes.git (push)
    ```

4. to work on something new, create a descriptively named branch off of master,
    and commit/push to the new branch, when it ready for merging, open a pull request.
5. to update local branch with upstream, see here: https://help.github.com/articles/syncing-a-fork/

see also: http://scottchacon.com/2011/08/31/github-flow.html

issue_title: Think about socket/stream sendall() high level concurrency protection
issue_body:
I looked at the high level async socket.sendall() trio 0.1.0 code and realized nothing would stop a user from calling sendall() from 2 tasks on the same socket, and having the individual lowlevel send()'s being intertwined, which is probably not what the user wants (ie fragmented/mashed sendall() data ordering).

Some options are:

- write some concurrency protection, raise an error
- write some documentation saying sendall() is not atomic
- make sendall atomic (via locking/parking lots, etc because I don't think we can safely disable checkpoints)

issue_title: Allow cancelling individual tasks
issue_body:
I am trying to write a server with trio. The main loop looks roughly like this:

```
async def accept_loop(nursery, sock, context):
    '''Accept connections and start tasks to handle them'''

    while True:
        (client_sock, addr) = await sock.accept()
        nursery.start_soon(process_requests, client_sock, context)
```

Now, I want to be able to shutdown the server cleanly. I could emit a cancel signal for the nursery,  but that wouldn't give the worker tasks a chance to finish processing the hcurrent request. Therefore, the workers check `context.should_terminate` after each request. By setting this flag and waiting a little, I can cleanly shut down the workers.

That leaves me with the problem of the stopping `accept_loop()`. I can't rely on a flag here, because `sock.accept` may block for a very long time. So I need to cancel this task. But if I do so while the worker tasks are still running, they will get terminated as well. And if I do it after the workers have shutdown, then I will keep accepting requests when I actually want to shut down.

It would make my life much easier if I could cancel a specific task, rather than the entire thread group. Is there a reason why `nursery.start_soon`  doesn't return something like curio's `Task` object which I can then use to cancel just this task?

issue_title: YAML validation fails for valid Python expressions that are invalid YAML
issue_body:
This is expected behavior, but falls short of our claim that users can write `any python expression` and is currently breaking the fbt example. 

Examples: 

**Function arguments**

`Value: sorted(last_24_hours.products_bought.extend(next_24_hours.products_bought), key=itemgetter(1), reverse=True)[:3]`

Running `blurr validate` fails with `DataGroups.3.Fields.0.Value: 'sorted(last_24_hours.products_bought.extend(next_24_hours.products_bought), key=itemgetter(1), reverse=True)[:3]' sets value using `=`.` even though the usage of = is correct within the sorted function

**Mapping values**
`Value: sorted(last_24_hours.products_bought.extend(next_24_hours.products_bought), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse==True)[:3]` is another way to write the sorted function but it is not a valid YAML. 

yamllint.com throws a validation error `(<unknown>): mapping values are not allowed in this context at line 82 column 103` - the culprit being `key=lambda x: x[1]`  (Blurr validation fails too but doesn't show the problem). 

There are likely to be many other situations where Python expressions will lead to failed YAML validation - do we need a bigger strategy for how to handle this?

issue_title: Question: Is oauthlib being stricter than OIDC spec, disallowing use of prompt=none without 'id_token_hint'?
issue_body:
According to the [OIDC spec](http://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#AuthRequest):

> id_token_hint
> OPTIONAL. ID Token previously issued by the Authorization Server being passed as a hint about the End-User's current or past authenticated session with the Client. If the End-User identified by the ID Token is logged in or is logged in by the request, then the Authorization Server returns a positive response; otherwise, it SHOULD return an error, such as login_required. When possible, an id_token_hint SHOULD be present when prompt=none is used and **an invalid_request error MAY be returned if it is not**; however, **the server SHOULD respond successfully when possible, even if it is not present**. The Authorization Server need not be listed as an audience of the ID Token when it is used as an id_token_hint value. 

(emphasis added)

Right now, the `oauthlib` library seems to be making that decision for the caller. The [OIDC validator raises an InvalidRequestError](https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/grant_types/openid_connect.py#L358) if `prompt=none` is sent without an accompanying `id_token_hint` parameter.

Is this what we want to be doing here? The spec leaves open the possibility for the server to identify the user by some other method (e.g. login cookie) even if `id_token_hint` is missing. In practice, I think that would be hard to do with oauthlib as currently written. Is the intention that the calling application could catch the `InvalidRequest` exception and handle it (as with the `LoginRequired` and `ConsentRequired` errors)? If so, how? The calling app would have to set some kind of value for `id_token_hint` to avoid hitting that check again.
